Biography
Dr. Mohamed Elhadidy is currently a professor of Biomedical Sciences, Director of Center for Genomics (CG), and Vice Director of Teaching Effectiveness Office (TEO) at Zewail City for science and technology at Zewail City of science and technology, Egypt. Dr. Elhadidy holds a DVM degree from Mansoura University in Egypt. He obtained his master’s degree in 2006 in Microbiology from Mansoura University and obtained his PhD (2010) in Microbiology from Virginia Tech University, USA. From 2011- 2017, Dr. Elhadidy served as a research scientist at School of Medicine, University of Saskatchewan, Canada; University of Bristol, UK; and University of Gent and Scientific Institute of Public health in Belgium. In 2018, he also served as a visiting professor at University of Bath, UK.
Dr. Elhadidy research program aims to develop a better understanding of the pathogenesis, ecology, evolutional dynamics, and transmission of bacterial zoonotic diseases through the implementation of different population genetics and comparative genomic studies of these bacterial pathogens. His most recent research activities implemented high throughput sequencing technologies and genomic epidemiology tools to gain crucialinsights into genetic determinants that play a role in virulence potential and antimicrobial resistance different zoonotic bacterial pathogens, including resistance determinants and their genomic signatures, to human. Implementation of such comprehensive resistome mapping and genomic surveillance is crucial to better understand the resistance dynamics.
Another aspect of research conducted at Dr. Elhadidy`s lab is to implement different innovative solutions to combat antimicrobial resistance, with a particular focus on the screening of natural compounds and different quorum inhibitors, loaded within organic nanoparticles, thus enhance their targeted delivery, improve bioavailability, facilitate the synergistic effects with other antibiotics, and revitalize their efficacy against multidrug-resistant pathogens.
Dr. Elhadidy research on genomic surveillance and developing alternative mechanisms to combat the escalating threat of antimicrobial resistance is interdisciplinary and involves the application of a variety of disciplines (including microbiology and microbial genomics, population genetics, molecular biology, genomics, evolution, nano-drug delivery systems, and bioinformatics) that contribute to the development of innovative and effective antimicrobial strategies.
Dr. Elhadidy is a current member of African Academy of Sciences, Egyptian Young academy of Sciences (EYAS), and National Microbiology Council a former exclusive committee member of the Global Young Academy, and a former member of committee, National Academy of Sciences, USA to develop framework of faculty members in MENA region and Egypt who are better able to teach issues related to the responsible conduct of science by combining tenets of active learning pedagogical techniques.

Obayya’s achievements have been acknowledged with numerous national and international accolades, including awards for research excellence, scientific creativity, and contributions to engineering sciences. Dr. Shaaban Khalil
Director of Center for Fundamental Physics
Professor, Center for Fundamental Physics
Email:
skhalil@zewailcity.edu.eg
Office Location / Building:
Academic Building
Biography
Prof. Shaaban Khalil is one of the prominent High Energy Physicists in the Middle East. He is well known by his outstanding contributions to understanding of possible new physics beyond the standard model of particle physics. Prof. Khalil is the founding Director of the Center for Fundamental Physics at Zewail City of Science and Technology in Egypt, since 2012. Additionally, he held the role of Director General of Research at Zewail City from 2015 to 2018. In 2015, Prof. Khalil's scholarly achievements led to his appointment as a tenured professor at Zewail City of Science and Technology, a distinction conferred upon him by Prof. Ahmed Zewail, following a comprehensive evaluation of his research, teaching, and service to the community by six external professors. In 1997, Khalil completed his PhD in Supersymmetry Phenomenology, followed by being awarded the Fulbright fellowship at the Bartol Research Institute, University of Delaware, USA. Between 1999 and 2004, Khalil occupied research and postdoctoral positions at the Physics Departments of Madrid University, Sussex University, and the Institute of Particle Physics Phenomenology at Durham University. His extensive contributions led to the award of a D.Sc. degree in 2006 from Ain Shams University, refereed by the Royal Society in the UK. In 2012-2013, Khalil was granted the Leverhulme visiting professorship at the Physics Department, University of Southampton, where he continues to serve as a visiting professor. Khalil is renowned for his significant contributions to unraveling new phenomena beyond the Standard Model of particle physics. With a prolific publication record exceeding 300 scientific papers in esteemed journals, his expertise extends to the theory of elementary particles, notably in Supersymmetry and the intersection of particle physics with cosmology. Prof. Khalil is also recognized as the co-author of two notable books: "Supersymmetry Beyond Minimality: From Theory to Experiment," published by CRC Press in the UK with ISBN 9781498756730 in 2017, and "Standard Model Phenomenology," published by CRC Press in the UK with ISBN 9780429443015 in 2022. Furthermore, he authored an Arabic book aimed at the public audience on particle physics, published by Andromeda Publishing in the UK with ISBN 978173981526. In 2017, Khalil took on the role of founding Editor-in-Chief of the Letters in High Energy Physics (LHEP) journal. Additionally, he serves as a reviewer for numerous international physics journals. He served as the founding team leader of the Egyptian Network of High Energy Physics (ENHEP) at the CMS experiment from 2009 to 2012, further solidifying his commitment to advancing scientific endeavors within Egypt and beyond. Khalil holds the distinction of being the founding President of the Arab Physical Society (ArPS). He is also a member of the African Academy of Sciences (AAS) and has served as the chair of the AAS Membership Advisory Committee in the field of Physical Sciences, as well as the chair of the Africa Synchrotron Initiative Committee. Furthermore, he has been a member of the Research Council for Basic Sciences at the Academy of Scientific Research and Technology since 2015. Khalil's research has garnered widespread recognition, leading to numerous accolades both locally and internationally. Among these honors are the State Appreciation Award in the field of basic sciences for the year 2021, the Egypt Award for Excellence in Basic Science in 2006, the Misr ElKheir Award for Most Cited Researchers in 2010, the Egyptian Award for Encouragement in 2000, the Shoman Award for Arab Physicists in 2001, and the International Award of Istanbul University in Science in 2004.

Biography
Dr. Ahmed Sayed Abd-Rabou Mohammed is a
Assistant Professor
in the Nanotechnology and Nanoelectronics Program at Zewail City of Science and Technology. He received his Ph.D. in Electrical Power and Machines Engineering from Ain Shams University in 2020, with guidance from an external supervisor at the University of Waterloo. His work emphasized the "Analysis and Optimal Design of Brushless DC Micromotors." Dr. Abd-Rabou began his professional journey in the industry, where he served as an R&D Electrical Motor Senior Designer at Misr Compressor Manufacture Co. ZMC from January 2007 to January 2013. This role laid the groundwork for his profound expertise in electrical motor design, manufacturing, and technological advancements, marking the start of over a 19 years of extensive experience in both industrial and academic spheres. During this period, he studied Electrical Motor Design in the UK (at Motor Design Ltd. and Glasgow University) and also trained at the R&D center of ACC Compressor Co. in Italy. With a robust foundation in engineering and a keen eye for innovation, Dr. Abd-Rabou has made significant contributions to the academic community through his teaching and research. He has developed and led courses across various domains, including Nanofabrication Vacuum and Equipment Technology, Micro/Nano Fabrication Techniques Lab, and Embedded & Digital Control systems design. His commitment to education is reflected in his supervision of groundbreaking graduate projects and postgraduation theses, focusing on cutting-edge topics such as soft robotics, AI applications in robotics, and advanced fabrication processes. Dr. Abd-Rabou's involvement in several collaborative projects with entities like the Egypt Space Agency highlights his commitment to fostering partnerships between academia and industry. He has been awarded grants from ITAC as both PI and Co-PI. Moreover, Dr. Abd-Rabou has been instrumental in establishing research tracks for the design and building of benchtop-scale equipment for CMOS fabrication. He has played a crucial role in developing and supervising labs, managing educational cleanrooms (of which he is a co-founder). His efforts in lab supervision have contributed to the program's ABET accreditation. Dr. Abd-Rabou's research interests are broad yet focused on impactful areas such as microfabrication equipment design, electrical and material characterization of electronic devices, and the optimization of electric motor designs. His work has been published in reputable journals, including IEEE Transactions and IET, contributing significantly to the fields of linear motors, micromotors, and solar concentrators. Research Interests:  Building of benchtop-scale equipment for CMOS fabrication  Micro Motor Design  Soft Robotics, Robotics Design  Embedded Systems and Control Systems Design  Design Optimization

Biography
I have approximately 20 years of experience as a particle physicist, with a strong background in experimental particle physics. My career began at the University of Geneva, where I worked on the ATLAS electromagnetic calorimeter at the Large Hadron Collider in CERN, Geneva, Switzerland during my Master's degree. During my PhD studies from 2008-2011, I was the main analyst for the Excited Lepton group (electron channel) and played a crucial role in initiating the analysis channel in ATLAS, contributing to the discovery of the Higgs boson in 2012. After completing my PhD in 2012, I returned to Cairo to work at Helwan University before joining Zewail City of Science and Technology as a member of their inaugural research team in February 2012. Since leaving the ATLAS experiment, I have been working with the CMS collaboration on the Gas Electron Multiplier (GEM) project and currently focus on Muon System data certification. My expertise lies in physics analysis, particularly in Beyond Standard Model (BSM) channels such as Mono-Z analysis, Higgs to diphoton analysis, dimuon analysis, and Excited lepton analysis. Additionally, I am a member of the MATHUSLA collaboration where I am involved in detector simulation using Geant4.

Biography
Dr. Amr Mohamed joined Zewail City of Science and Technology (ZC) on January 1st, 2014. He is currently an Associate Professor in the Physics of Universe Program at the University of Science and Technology at Zewail City. His research interests include Computational Physics and Monte Carlo methods development. Dr. Mohamed also served as an Adjunct Professor at the Nuclear and Radiation Engineering Department, Alexandria University. Prior to joining ZC, Dr. Mohamed worked as a Research Scientist in the Nuclear Engineering Division at Argonne National Laboratory, USA. Dr. Mohamed obtained his Bachelor’s degree in Nuclear Engineering from Alexandria University in 1989, his Master’s and Ph.D degrees in Nuclear Engineering from The Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) in 1992 and 1994, respectively. He is a member of the American Nuclear Society (ANS), American Physical Society (APS), Alpha Nu Sigma Honor Society and the Egyptian Engineers Syndicate

Biography
Dr. Mahmoud Abdelaziz is an Assistant Professor at the University of Science and Technology in Zewail City, Egypt. He teaches several courses related to statistical inference and data analysis, information theory, signal processing, control systems, communication systems, and introductory computer science. He received the Ph.D. degree with distinction in Signal Processing and Electrical Communications Engineering from Tampere University of Technology, Finland, in 2017. He received an award from the Industrial Research Fund of Tampere in recognition of his excellent and high-grade Doctoral dissertation. After obtaining the Ph.D. degree, he worked as a Postdoctoral Researcher at Tampere University and as a Lecturer at the British University in Egypt (BUE). He received the B.Sc. (with honors) and M.Sc. degrees in Electronics and Electrical Communications Engineering from Cairo University, Egypt, in 2006 and 2011, respectively. His research interests include statistical and digital signal processing, machine learning, and telecommunications. He also has industry experience in the implementation side of signal processing algorithms, in particular real-time signal processing. From 2007 to 2012, He worked as a signal processing engineer then as an embedded software engineer at Newport Media Inc. (which was acquired by Atmel then by Microchip Technology). He has also worked in the field of mobile communications at Etisalat Egypt.

Biography
Dr. Mohamad Samir A. Eid is an Assistant Professor in Cybersecurity at the Communications and Information Engineering (CIE) Program at Zewail City in Egypt. He is recognized as a passionate student-centered educator continually seeking to improve the students' learning process. He earned his Ph.D. degree in Cybersecurity from the University of Tokyo in 2017. During his Ph.D., he has received several funding grants including the one from The University of Tokyo special incentives program for excellent candidates and five more grants from governmental and non-governmental entities in Japan. Dr. Eid's research publications and interests are in the areas of Network Security, DDoS Mitigation, Intrusion Detection, and Access Control. In addition, Dr. Eid is an expert in academic accreditation and in curriculum design and construction, teaching topics including Cryptography, Network Security, Computer Networks, and Digital Design. He also acts as the champion of the CIE program, and the head of the CIE accreditation committee. Further, he is a member of the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) professional association and the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM)

Biography
Mohamed Maher Ata is an Associate professor at School of Computational Sciences and Artificial intelligence (CSAI), Zewail city of science and Technology, Egypt. He has received his PhD from the faculty of Engineering, Electrical communication & electronics department, Tanta University, Egypt with the cooperation of Regina University in Canada. His research area of interest was utilized in the field of signal processing, image processing, Multimedia, machine and deep learning, video processing, and computer vision. He has published more than 40 research articles (SJR indexed-ISI indexed) in the state-ofthe art of Computer Vision, Artificial Intelligence (AI), Optimization, Deep and machine learning, Biomedical Engineering, Astrophysics, Electrical Communications, Bioinformatics, Encryption, Cyphering and Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS).

Biography
Shaimaa Ali Mohamed is an Assistant Professor at Zewail City for Science and Technology, Egypt. Shaimaa conducted her PhD. study at Linz Institute for Organic Solar Cell (LIOS), Johannes Kepler University Linz, Austria through the Africa-North Exchange Program (ANEX) fellowship funded by the International Center for Theoritical Physics (ICTP) and the African Newtwork for Solar Energy (ANSOLE). Shaimaa obtained her PhD degree in 2015 in the field of organic and quantum dots Photovoltic. Her main research interst is organic and quantum dots Photovoltaic, Perovskite solar cells, printed organic electronics and photonics, flexible transparent conducting electrodes, oraganic light emitting diods (OLED) and supercapacitors. Shaimaa had the chance to present her research findings at many international conferences worldwide and to travel to different countries in Europe and Africa. Her academic contributions have yielded several high impact peer-reviewed scholarly scientific publications, and awards including the Best Contribution Prize, Cape Town, South Africa 2013, Best Poster and Oral Prize, Summer School for young Scientists on Renewable Energies in Africa, Arusha, Tanzania 2015, young research award, Thessaloniki, Greece 2015 and selected as the Best ANSOLE fellow for 2016. She has established the organic electronic lab at Zewail City, Egypt. Reletaed to her research, she is actively ienrolled in three projects: The STDF Egypt-Italy Joint fund Project “ Self-Organized nanostructures for solar energy harvesting as a Co-PI” and team member inNewton Musharafa STDF project “Highly-efficient nano-structure solar cells for energy security” and the Solar Energy Alliance (ASRT) project.

Biography
Omar Zaki was born in Assiout, Egypt in 1974. He received his BSc in chemistry from Assiout University in 1996. In 1998, Zaki joined the Egyptian Mineral Resource Authority, Ministry of Petroleum. Zaki was responsible for measuring and determining of major and minor elements in all types of rocks, ores, clays, minerals, water and wastewater by using different techniques and instrumentation. During his analytical career, Zaki participated in many projects such as "Cairo Air Improvement Project" and "Assessment and Extraction of Gold from Egyptian Waste Gold Mines". In 2002, he earned his MSc in chemistry from Helwan University. Zaki served as lab instructor as a part-time faculty member, faculty of engineering (Shoubra campus), Banha University from 2003 to 2005. From 2003 to May 2012, Zaki joined the American University in Cairo (AUC), as a teaching assistant in chemistry department. From 2007 to May 2012, Zaki joined Yousef Jameel Science and Technology Research Center (YJ-STRC), the American University in Cairo (AUC), as a research assistant, working within the surface chemistry group under Professor Jehane Ragai and Dr. Adham Ramadan. Performed research work about synthesis of catalysis (primarily different transition metal oxide), surface characterization, as well as different properties (e.g. ion exchange, surface acidity, etc.), which bearing on the catalytic properties. In 2012, He received his PhD in chemistry from Cairo University with external examiners from North Carolina State University, Professor Keith Gubbins and Université de Provence, Professor Jean Rouquerol respectively. In June 2012, Zaki joined Zewail City of Science and Technology. Zaki is currently a lecturer at Zewail University

Aerospace Engineering
School of Engineering
The Aerospace Engineering major is a program aimed at providing the future graduates with the ability to fulfill the engineering tasks associated with air and space travel. The emphasis is on multidisciplinary education. The student is exposed to a range of topics covering basic math, physics and mechanics, engineering structures, fluid mechanics and aerodynamics, control engineering and robotics and propulsion. Additional courses on sensors, electronics, software and system engineering provide the student with a well-rounded approach to engineering. The graduate would thus have the reach and flexibility to start a career in the air transport or space industries or associated research centers. The program strongly emphasizes hands-on design experience and innovation. This is highlighted in the two-semester graduation project where students demonstrate their mastery of the abilities acquired throughout the program.
Accreditation Information
University of Science and Technology - Zewail City is
officially accredited by the Supreme Council of Universities in Egypt.
Director of Aerospace Engineering Program
Knowledge and ability to apply fundamental mathematical and physical concepts for the solution of engineering problems
Ability to design and create aerospace subsystems in the context of integrated projects
Skills necessary for effectively working as an individual and as a team member
Skills for scientific thinking and continuous learning
Knowledge of existing technology as well as trends for future developments in technology that support their engineering careers.
An ability to identify, formulate, and solve complex engineering problems by applying principles of engineering, science, and mathematics.
An ability to apply engineering design to produce solutions that meet specified needs with consideration of public health, safety, and welfare, as well as global, cultural, social, environmental, and economic factors.
An ability to communicate effectively with a range of audiences.
An ability to recognize ethical and professional responsibilities in engineering situations and make informed judgments, which must consider the impact of engineering solutions in global, economic, environmental, and societal contexts.
An ability to function effectively on a team whose members together provide leadership, create a collaborative and inclusive environment, establish goals, plan tasks, and meet objectives.
An ability to develop and conduct appropriate experimentation, analyze and interpret data, and use engineering judgment to draw conclusions.
An ability to acquire and apply new knowledge as needed, using appropriate learning strategies.
An ability to understand and apply the fundamentals of orbital mechanics, space environment, attitude determination and control, telecommunications, space structures, rocket propulsion, aerodynamics, aeronautic propulsion, and autonomous systems.
An ability to conduct research in one of the fields of study of Aerospace Engineering.
An ability to perform programming tasks to solve basic problems using one or more of the modern programming tools.
An ability to utilize locally available resources to solve technological problems.
Program Educational Objectives Mapping to Student Outcomes
1. University Requirements (18 Cr)
University Requirements provide UST students with skills and knowledge to develop an intellectual, well-rounded, and successful personal identity. University Requirements is a set of General Education courses (Humanities, Social Sciences, etc.), which are designed to enrich students with an appreciative understanding of the natural and cultural environments in which they live and their roles in the society and community services.
As shown in the below table, all ENGR students are required to complete 8 Cr of specific General Education Compulsory courses and 10 Cr of General Education courses that are program-specific. Students must refer to their program curriculum for how to fulfil these 10 Cr.

The aim of School Requirements is to provide ENGR students with skills and knowledge essential to develop a successful graduate. School Requirements include:
Math, basic science, and basic engineering topics that are essential to all ENGR students.
A successful completion of an internship to enable ENGR students to practice skills related to their field of specialization in a real-life workplace environment.
A successful completion of a Senior Design Project in which students integrate, apply, and demonstrate their acquired knowledge and skills.

Biomedical Sciences
School of Science
The Biomedical Science major is designed to provide students with basic and applied knowledge in the field of biology as it applies to health and disease.
In the first two years, students complete a common core foundation of basic biology in an interdisciplinary approach. The students will then choose from four concentrations: Molecular and Cell Biology, Medical Sciences, Computational Biology and Genomics, and Drug Design and Development. Courses in each concentration cover specialized knowledge in the field, technical electives, and a research thesis project. Upon graduation, students will be prepared for diverse jobs in the largely expanding biotechnology field, in both the public and private sectors of pharmaceuticals, medical, diagnostics, infectious diseases and bioinformatics. They would also be able to join research institutes and universities, continue postgraduate or professional degrees, or pursue leadership positions in their fields.
Accreditation Information
National Accreditation
University of Science and Technology - Zewail City is
officially accredited by the Supreme Council of Universities in Egypt.
International Accreditation
The Bachelor of Science degree program in Biomedical Sciences is accredited by the Applied and Natural Science Accreditation Commission of ABET, the global accreditor of college and university programs in applied and natural science, computing, engineering, and engineering technology.
Director of Biomedical Sciences Program
Employ essential knowledge of specific fields of biomedical sciences via research-based education to enable graduates to fulfill the needs of the evolving job market in the biomedical sciences fields.
Apply skills and knowledge of research-based education in academic careers that seek to create and spread knowledge in the field of biomedical sciences.
Implement creative and analytical solutions for issues related to health and biomedical challenges in the society and in the industry.
Apply multidisciplinary approach to complex problems in drug design, cell therapy, infectious diseases, and computational approaches to health issues, clinical laboratories and biomedical start-up companies.
Develop a collaborative network to integrate ethical, environmentally sound approaches to sustainable scientific practices.
By the time of graduation, students should attain the following SOs:
An ability to identify, formulate, and solve broadly-defined technical or scientific problems by applying knowledge of mathematics and science and/or technical topics to areas relevant to the discipline.
An ability to formulate or design a system, process, procedure or material to meet desired needs.
An ability to develop and conduct experiments or test hypotheses, analyze and interpret data and use scientific judgment to draw conclusions.
An ability to communicate effectively with a range of audiences.
An ability to understand ethical and professional responsibilities and the impact of technical and/or scientific solutions in global, economic, environmental, and societal contexts.
An ability to function effectively on teams that establish goals, plan tasks, meet deadlines, and analyze risk and uncertainty.
An ability to apply fundamental concepts in biomedical sciences to identify, evaluate and analyze problems that impact human health, and develop innovative approaches to solve emerging challenges in the field.
Program Educational Objectives Mapping to Student Outcomes
1. Degree Requirements for B. Sc. In Biomedical Sciences
A Student who intends to major in Biomedical Sciences must submit a Major Declaration Form upon completion of 34 credit hours of the Foundation Year. A total of 130 credits is required for the bachelor’s degree in Biomedical Sciences Program. Students should consult their advisor on a regular basis to ensure the prerequisites for their university requirements, School requirements, major requirements, concentration requirements and electives are fulfilled.
**No foundation courses could be delayed beyond the 2nd year for any students

The aim of university requirements is to provide UST students with skills and knowledge to develop an intellectual, well-rounded and successful personal identity. University requirements is a set of Humanities and Social Sciences courses which are designed to enrich students with an appreciative understanding of the natural and cultural environments in which they live and their roles in the society and community services.

Biotechnology
School of Science
PROGRAM DESCRIPTION
Biotechnology major is designed to provide students with fundamental and applied knowledge in life sciences and biotechnology, with a strong emphasis on applications in human health and disease. The program highlights
revolutionary biotechnological and digital technologies that are reshaping healthcare and related industries
, including genomics, artificial intelligence, and advanced molecular technologies. During the first two years, students complete an interdisciplinary core curriculum in the life sciences that establishes a solid scientific foundation. In the later years, students pursue specialized coursework in areas such as nanobiotechnology, point-of-care diagnostics, precision medicine, computational biology, and applications of artificial intelligence in biotechnology. These courses are complemented by technical electives and a research thesis project that provides hands-on experience with modern biotechnology techniques.
Potential Job Market
Upon graduation, students will be prepared to work in biotechnology sectors, including biopharmaceutical and vaccine development, molecular diagnostics, genomics laboratories, biomedical research centers, and healthcare biotechnology companies. Graduates may also pursue postgraduate studies or research careers in biotechnology and related fields.
Acquire hands-on training in the essential skills, laboratory methods, and experimental techniques used in biotechnology.
Apply principles of biotechnology and modern laboratory technologies to develop innovative solutions in life science applications.
Utilize advanced biotechnological tools such as genomics, bioinformatics, molecular engineering, and computational approaches to analyze biological systems and support technological innovation.
Translate scientific discoveries into practical biotechnological applications, including diagnostics, therapeutics, and biotechnology-driven solutions.
Integrate interdisciplinary knowledge from life sciences, data science, and biotechnology to address complex scientific and technological challenges.
Demonstrate professional, ethical, and collaborative practices that promote responsible biotechnology research, innovation, and technological advancement.

Communications and Information Engineering
School of Engineering
Communications and Information Engineering (CIE) is at the heart of the industrial 4.0 revolution that impacts everyone's live. New intelligent systems that are interconnected, embedded, and controlled are continually evolving. There is a need for experts who can develop and operate such intelligent systems. The CIE program offers a combination of knowledge about electronics, communications technologies, control, embedded systems, and machine learning. The program offers a large proportion of electives that enable the student to tailor their education in one of four areas: communication technologies, artificial intelligence, high performance computing, and robotics.
Teaching is conducted in a combination of lectures, practice, and hands on lab work and course projects in addition to the capstone project at the final year. Moreover, CIE program puts special emphasis on topics essential for technological development such as project management, presentation skills, entrepreneurship, and business development.
Accreditation Information
National Accreditation
University of Science and Technology - Zewail City is
officially accredited by the Supreme Council of Universities in Egypt.
International Accreditation
The Bachelor of Science degree program in Communications and information Engineering (CIE) is accredited by the Engineering Accreditation Commission of ABET, the global accreditor of college and university programs in applied and natural science, computing, engineering, and engineering technology.
The CIE program nurtures an academic environment that contributes effectively to empower its graduates towards having the choice, talents, and knowledge to attain the following educational objectives within three to five years of graduation to:
Pursue a diverse range of careers as engineers in the field of communications and information engineering and related fields.
Pursue advanced degrees in local and international graduate programs in communications and information engineering and interdisciplinary areas to emerge as thought leaders, researchers, experts, and educators.
Be lifelong learners and innovators in ever-changing global economic and technological environments of the 21st century.
Assume entrepreneurial and leadership positions in the field of communications and information engineering and related fields.
Student Outcomes describe what students are expected to be able to do by the time of graduation. The CIE program adopts the Student Outcomes of the ABET Engineering Accreditation Commission. By the time of graduation, CIE students attain the following outcomes:
General Engineering Student Outcomes
An ability to identify, formulate, and solve complex engineering problems by applying principles of engineering, science, and mathematics.
An ability to apply engineering design to produce solutions that meet specified needs with consideration of public health, safety, and welfare, as well as global, cultural, social, environmental, and economic factors.
An ability to communicate effectively with a range of audiences.
An ability to recognize ethical and professional responsibilities in engineering situations and make informed judgments, which must consider the impact of engineering solutions in global, economic, environmental, and societal contexts.
An ability to function effectively on a team whose members together provide leadership, create a collaborative and inclusive environment, establish goals, plan tasks, and meet objectives.
An ability to develop and conduct appropriate experimentation, analyze and interpret data, and use engineering judgment to draw conclusions.
An ability to acquire and apply new knowledge as needed, using appropriate learning strategies.
Program Educational Objectives Mapping to Student Outcomes

Provide a regular channel of communication between the program and relevant sections of industry and workforce demand.
Ensure that that School receives appropriate, and expert advice on the curriculum.
Offering Recruitment opportunities for CIE graduates.
Offering College/Industrial internships for CIE students
Supporting students’ graduation projects.
Guest lectures are delivered by IAB members.

CYBER SECURITY
School of Computational Sciences & Artificial Intelligence
Cybersecurity (CYB) is a program where a core body of knowledge in secure computing, network and systems security, ethical hacking, cryptography, digital forensics, cloud and IoT security, and risk management is utilized to safeguard information assets and ensure the resilience of digital infrastructures. As cyber threats grow in sophistication and scale, the demand for cybersecurity professionals is rapidly increasing and is projected to remain high both locally and globally. Careers in cybersecurity consistently rank among the most in-demand and highest-paying roles in the technology sector.
The CYB program prepares graduates with both theoretical foundations and practical experience needed to protect and defend computing systems and networks. In the first two years, students gain essential knowledge in computing, security principles, secure programming, system administration, and incident response fundamentals. During the last two years, students engage in advanced and specialized coursework such as penetration testing, security operations, threat intelligence, cloud and IoT security, cyber law, and compliance frameworks, enabling them to contribute effectively to national and enterprise cybersecurity needs.
Potential Job Market
With digital transformation accelerating across all industries, cybersecurity has become essential to safeguarding national security, businesses, and communities.
The CYB program equips students with a powerful combination of theoretical and hands‐on skills in:
Ethical hacking & penetration testing
Network & systems defence
Digital forensics & incident response
Cloud & IoT security
Cryptography & risk management
Graduates are prepared to secure modern digital infrastructures and respond effectively to evolving threats.
Students completing the program are positioned for top cybersecurity roles such as:
Graduates are needed in virtually every sector, including:
Cybersecurity Specialist / Associate
Threat Intelligence Analyst
Cloud Security Engineer
IoT/OT Security Specialist
Finance & banking
Government & defense
Healthcare
Telecom & critical infrastructure
Global tech companies

Academics
Minors
A Minor consists of a specified set of courses in a particular discipline or field that a student optionally chooses outside the field covered by the Program the student is enrolled in. UST students may pursue one or more of the minors offered by any UST school.
Students should note that electing a Minor is optional and its credits do not count toward the credit requirements for the Program the student is enrolled in. Students should also note that offering courses for Minors in a given academic year is decided based on available UST resources and the needs and plans of the Programs offering the Minors.
Offered by BMS
The minor in BMS is designed to provide the students with broad knowledge in the Biomedical Sciences fields. The minor courses are selected from the BMS program concentrations of Molecular Cell Biology, Medical Sciences, Computational Biology, and Genomics. and Drug Design and Development. An additional elective course enables the students to gain more knowledge in the field of their choice.

Through the selected courses, students will gain a strong foundation in information engineering principles. In addition, students can choose from a selection of specialized courses to further enhance their expertise in this dynamic field. This minor provides a valuable skill set that complements various majors. Enhance your academic profile by gaining proficiency in programming, data analysis, and machine learning, essential tools in today's technology-driven world. This minor equips you with cross-disciplinary capabilities to innovate and excel in your chosen field.
Students in the DSAI program are not eligible to declare this minor because it overlaps with their major field of study.

The minor in Nanoelectronics Engineering offers students a comprehensive journey into the realm of nanoelectronics and its applications. The Minor in Artificial Intelligence and Big Data is designed to qualify students (with software development background) to develop software applications, to carry out scientific research in the fields of Machine and deep learning, and to be able to analyze big data to help decision makers.
Students in the CIE program are not eligible to declare this minor because it overlaps with their major field of study
. This minor is 15 credit hours and is divided into three parts:
Part 1: Basic concepts of Machine and deep learning (6 Cr). It covers most common learning algorithms such as Support Vector Machine (SVM), Neural Networks (NN), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), and Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) with many variants of those algorithms.
Part 2: Applications of Deep learning in Computer Vision and Natural Language Processing (6 Cr). it covers many applications such as Objects Detection, Localization and Classification, Face Recognition, Image Segmentation, Text Summarization, Sentiment Analysis of tweets, and Transformers (the core of Chat GPT).
Part 3: Basics of Big Data Analytics (3 Cr). It covers challenges of Big Data and how to deal with it. Many common analytics tools are explored and applied on real world data. By successfully passing this minor, students will be eligible to apply for jobs in AI based companies, to join research projects in AI field, and to work as Junior Data Analyst in big firms and enterprises in Telecom and Banking Sectors.
Courses:

DSAI 308 Deep Learning
DSAI 352 Computer Vision
DSAI 353 Natural language processing

Nanoscience is a multidisciplinary field that explores the behavior and manipulation of different materials at the nanoscale, which is roughly 1 to 100 nanometers in size. A minor in Nanoscience is an academic degree that allows students with a foundational understanding of the principles, theories, and applications of nanoscience. The minor degree in Nanoscience typically complements a major degree in a related field such as chemistry, biology, physics, materials science, pharmacy, or engineering. It offers students an opportunity to gain specialized knowledge and skills in nanoscience while pursuing their primary area of study.
The curriculum of a minor degree in Nanoscience includes a combination of three compulsory core courses and two elective courses. The compulsory core courses cover fundamental concepts in nanoscience and the unique properties and phenomena exhibited by materials at the nanoscale, as well as the nanomaterials’ fabrication techniques, and the fundamental characterizations at the nanoscale. The two elective courses (NANOSC 300 or 400 level courses or from the electives list) allow students to explore specific areas of nanoscience in greater depth.

2- NANOSC 302 Modern Characterization Techniques I

NANOSC 301 Spectroscopy of Nanomaterials

5- NANOSC 300 / 400 level course

Mission
AAU mission is to provide professional support to UST students from admission to graduation, ensuring academic success and personal development. Academic Advising Unit (AAU) is designed to ease students’ transition from high school to university. This is achieved through educating students about the required skills and knowledge to enhance their academic success, capitalizing on the importance of choosing the right field of study, and familiarizing them with the available academic programs, and with the available personal support on campus.
Vision
Providing UST students with institutional support and direction about academic, social, or personal matters.
Students Regulations
Each student in UST should be assigned to a specific Academic Advisor. Academic Advisors duties are to counsel their advisees throughout their journey as undergraduate students. Academic Advisors help their advisees to define their goals of the study and targeted career, to understand their requirements, restrictions and policies, to plan their academic tracks, and generally to overcome any obstacle –socially and/or personally- that might hinder their progress.
Students MUST visit their advisor to
Select/change their track.
Select their major.
Select/change their concentration (if any).
Select elective courses.
Students may need to visit their advisor to
Confirm the academic requirements for their degree.
Make sure they are enrolled in the right courses.
Review UST policies and procedures.
Explore major and career options.
Plan future steps in their education.
Discover campus resources and engagement opportunities.
Check their academic progress.
Make a connection between challenging academic work and the students’ future success.
Approach
AAU should apply the following methods and techniques to ensure achieving the learning outcomes.
1- One-to-One Advising Sessions
2- Group Advising Sessions
Academic orientation
Major fairs and seminars
Small groups meetings
Peer advising
3- Communication via email
Open communication channels to detect problems as early as possible
4- Online tools
Accessible advising link on SSS
Accessible students’ academic records
Appointment reservation system
Advising hold to ensure the obligatory meetings
Advising assessment survey for professional development purposes

Research at Zewail City
Zewail City of Science and Technology is dedicated to fostering a culture of rigorous inquiry and groundbreaking discovery. Our esteemed research community, comprising world-renowned scholars and visionary researchers, is committed to advancing knowledge and addressing global challenges through interdisciplinary collaboration and innovation. With state of the art facilities and cutting-edge technologies at their disposal, our researchers push the boundaries of scientific exploration across a diverse spectrum of disciplines, from fundamental science to applied research with tangible societal impact. Through a steadfast dedication to excellence, integrity, and societal relevance, Zewail City stands at the front of scientific research, driving transformative change and shaping the future of academic knowledge.
Vision
Zewail City of Science, Technology, and Innovation was established with the vision of becoming a world-class scientific and educational institution that fosters innovation, research, and development across various disciplines, to lead the scientific development in Egypt.
Mission
The mission of Zewail City is multi-faceted and includes:
Promoting Excellence in Research:
Zewail City is dedicated to conducting cutting-edge research in many fields such as Nanotechnology, Material Science, Nanoscience, Biomedical Science and Information Technology, among others. Through its state-of-the-art facilities and collaborations with leading institutions worldwide, the university aims to push the boundaries of scientific knowledge and contribute to global advancements.
Fostering Innovation and Entrepreneurship:
We seeks to foster a culture of innovation and entrepreneurship among its students, faculty, and researchers. Through various initiatives, such as incubators, startup competitions, and industry partnerships, the university aims to support the development of innovative solutions to real-world challenges and promote economic growth and sustainability.
Advancing Sustainable Development:
Through interdisciplinary research and collaborations, the university endeavors to develop sustainable solutions that improve the quality of life for people around the world while safeguarding the planet for future generations.
Promoting Collaboration and Diversity:
Our Community values collaboration and diversity as essential drivers of innovation and progress. The City actively seeks partnerships with academic institutions, research organizations, and industry stakeholders, both locally and internationally, to leverage expertise and resources for mutual benefit.
Overall, Zewail City's vision and mission reflect its dedication to advancing scientific knowledge, educating future generations of leaders, fostering innovation and entrepreneurship, promoting sustainable development, and fostering collaboration and diversity to address global challenges and make a positive impact on society.
Strategic Objectives
Zewail City of Science, Technology, and Innovation set strategic objectives aligned with its overarching vision of becoming a leading center for scientific research and innovation in the Middle East. These strategic objectives encompass various facets of the institution's mission and goals:
Research Excellence:
Zewail City aims to achieve excellence in scientific research across multiple disciplines. This objective involves conducting high-impact research that addresses fundamental scientific questions and addresses pressing societal challenges. The city strives to publish its research findings in top-tier peer-reviewed journals and conferences, thereby contributing to the global scientific community.
Interdisciplinary Collaboration:
The City fosters interdisciplinary collaboration among researchers from different fields. By promoting cross-disciplinary research initiatives, the institution seeks to leverage diverse perspectives and expertise to tackle complex problems. Interdisciplinary collaboration is seen as essential for driving innovation and generating transformative solutions.
Technology Transfer and Innovation:
Technology transfer and innovation are integral components of the institution's strategy to create societal impact and contribute to economic development. The city collaborates with industrial partners, startups, and entrepreneurs to facilitate the commercialization of research findings and the development of innovative technologies.
Regional and Global Engagement:
Zewail City seeks to enhance its visibility and impact through regional and global engagement. This involves establishing partnerships with other academic institutions, research organizations, and industry stakeholders both locally and internationally. By fostering collaboration and knowledge exchange, the institution aims to position itself as a hub for scientific cooperation and innovation in the Middle East region and beyond.

Helmy Institute for Medical Sciences (HIMS)
Helmy Institute for Medical Sciences (HIMS) is a leading research institution dedicated to advancing health and medical knowledge through a multidisciplinary approach. It encompasses 5 specialized centers, including :
The Center for Aging and Associated Diseases (CAAD)
the Center for Genomics (CG)
the Center for Microbiology and Phage Therapy
the Center of Excellence for Stem Cells and Regenerative Medicine
the Center of Scientific Excellence for Food Research and Analysis (cSEFRA)
Together, these centers position HIMS at the forefront of medical research and innovation, contributing significantly to societal health and well-being.
Center for Aging and Associated Diseases
Why does a mouse live for two years while an elephant lives for sixty, even though both animals consume the same amount of oxygen during their lifetime? Why do some whales live for 200-300 years while salmon die immediately after they reproduce at two to eight years of age? How do metabolic rates determine the lifespan of all organisms and why are birds a clear exception? Why do some humans age slowly and maintain their cognitive function, while others go through accelerated physical and mental decline while aging? Is it possible to slow down the pace of aging?
These are some of the fundamental questions we are attempting to answer at the Center for Aging and Associated Diseases (CAAD). The center is equipped with state-of-the-art facilities and capitalizes on internationally recognized investigators and brilliant students to explore the mechanisms of aging and degenerative diseases.
Center for Genomics
The Center for Genomics (CG) is a leading research center at Zewail City of Science and Technology (ZC) that employs an interdisciplinary research approach to better understand how genomes function in health and disease. We apply many tools used in genomics research, including physiological, cellular, imaging, molecular, and computational techniques, with the aim of translating genomics research into new methods of treating and diagnosing disease with a tangible impact on society.
A new pillar recently developed in CG is to use high throughput sequencing technologies and analysis pipelines to better understand the integration of microbial infection strategies, host cellular functions, and host defense mechanisms in the pathogenesis of microbial diseases.
[CG Facilities](
Center for Microbiology and Phage Therapy
The center focuses on the potential of using phages and their new biotechnology products such as Endolysin in the treatment of bacterial infections in food, veterinary and human.
Center of Excellence for Stem Cells and Regenerative Medicine
The Center of Excellence for stem Cells and Regenerative Medicine at Zewail city was established in 2013 by a grant to Dr. Nagwa El-Badri from the Egyptian Science and Technology Development Fund in 2013 and FLUG grant in 2023.
Center of Scientific Excellence for Food Research and Analysis
cSEFRA focuses on the establishment of rapid and accurate testing services for the analysis of food, water, and agricultural products. Food products are evaluated for their chemical and microbial quality in accordance with ISO standards.

NBE Institute for Nanoscience and Informatics (INI)
NBE Institute for Nanoscience and Informatics (INI) is a multidisciplinary research hub dedicated to advancing the fields of nanoscience and nanotechnology. It encompasses several specialized centers, including :
The Center for Materials Science(CMS), the Center for Nanoelectronics and Devices(CND), the Center for Nanotechnology (CNT), additional Center for Photonics and Smart Materials, Lastly the Certified Reference Materials (CRM) Center.
Together, these centers position INI as a leader in scientific research and technological advancement, contributing to a sustainable and knowledge-based economy.
Center for Material Science
The Center for Materials Science (CMS) is a multidisciplinary research center that focuses on developing novel materials for applications in demanding technologies, especially those that can contribute to alleviating current problems in the health, energy and water needs of the country. The CMS research scope is broad and involves the design and synthesis of nanomaterials for improved health and environment conditions. The applications include novel smart drug and gene delivery vehicles, nanostructures for tissue engineering, regenerative medicine research and water treatment materials. The development of functional materials for energy and industrial applications focuses on hydrogen storage, gas absorption and separation, heterogeneous catalysis, and solar-to-chemical energy conversions.
Center for Nanoelectronics and Devices
In 2011, the consumer electronics industry generated revenues in excess of $1 trillion. Despite market fluctuations, these revenues have been growing steadily over the past four decades. Local and regional shares in this industry have, thus far, been very limited. However, with correct planning and vision, this industry can become significantly developed, which will serve to increase national income and create local and regional high-tech jobs. This development will help elevate the region’s advancement and aid in its transition to a knowledge-based economy.
The CND includes more than ten distinguished senior researchers and 25 junior researchers working on many exciting projects. These projects are being conducted in collaboration with top international research groups across the globe, in addition to top multinational companies including Intel and Mentor Graphics.
Center for Nanotechnology
The Center for Nanotechnology (CNT) is a leading research center in micro/nanotechnology to advance scientific knowledge and foster innovation. The CNT conducts cutting-edge research in different related fields to nanotechnology through collaborations with Zewail City research centers, Egyptian universities, and global research centers. The CNT aims to drive advancements in micro/nanosystems and to be a recognized center of expertise in nanotechnology.
Center for Photonics and Smart Materials
Scientific Excellence for Certified Reference Materials
The Certified Reference Materials (CRM) Center at Zewail City is dedicated to supporting Egypt's strategic industries by producing high-quality, locally-made certified reference materials. With a strong reliance on CRMs for the accurate identification and quantification of raw materials and products, industries such as pharmaceuticals and various official authorities have long depended on foreign suppliers. This dependence drains valuable hard currency and adds economic strain. The CRM Center brings together Egyptian expertise in synthetic chemistry, spectroscopic analysis, catalysis, and accreditation, leveraging state-of-the-art facilities to foster a self-sustaining CRM industry within the country.

Center for Fundamental Physics
The Center for Fundamental Physics (CFP) at Zewail City of Science and Technology was established in February 2012. The CFP aims to foster world-class research in theoretical physics through building and growing a community of scientists dedicated to high-level research. Also to attract highly motivated young researchers as well as qualified leading scientists from around the world with the aim of strengthening the foundation of physical sciences in Egypt.

Institute for Imaging and Visualization (IIV)
Institute for Imaging and Visualization (IIV) is a cutting-edge research
institution dedicated to advancing the fields of imaging and structural
biotechnology. It encompasses
the Center for Imaging and Microscopy (CIM)Additionally, the Center for X-Ray Determination of the Structure of Matter (CXDS)
These centers position IIV as a leader in imaging technology and structural research , contributing significantly to scientific advancements in Egypt and the global community.
Center for Imaging and Microscopy
The Center for Imaging and Microscopy (CIM) was established in November 2012. Several Egyptians are now receiving training at leading scientific institutions, such as Caltech and the Max Planck Institutes, for the purpose of working at this high tech and unique facility. This training emphasizes the importance the center gives to acquiring the expertise needed to operate sophisticated instruments involving advanced electron microscopy imaging and laser spectroscopy.
CIM is equipped to enable imaging of nano-objects with atomic-scale-level of accuracy, as well as the ability to track movements of molecules and particles on the time scale of a millionth of a billionth of a second, the femtosecond. This center will play a pivotal role in research at Zewail City by working closely with its other research centers and by interacting with other research centers and universities in Egypt.
Because the center involves the latest in high-speed electron imaging (200 keV) with four dimensional (4-D) capabilities — the 3-D of space and 1-D of time, the developments at Caltech, which resulted in a number of patents to and publications by Dr. Zewail, will be a source of collaboration between the two institutions.
The first 4-D electron microscope in the region will be operational in 2014 and will be part of the city’s facilities in imaging and nanoscience and nanotechnology.
**Some details are given in the following references:**
A. H. Zewail, "Four-Dimensional Electron Microscopy", Science, 187, 328 (2010).
A. H. Zewail, "Filming the Invisible in 4D", Scientific American, 303, 74 (2010).
Center for X-Ray Determination of the Structure of Matter
All organisms depend on pathways involving interactions between proteins, DNA, and RNA. These interactions rely on the specific molecular structures involved. Therefore, studying how biological molecules fold is essential for enhancing their functions. Understanding molecular folding allows us to develop solutions for treating diseases, improving agricultural yields, refining medications, advancing diagnostic techniques, and more.
At CXDS Center, our primary focus is on structural biotechnology. Initially, we prioritize a comprehensive approach that combines computational analysis with empirical validation through cloning and functional assays to unravel the complex structural dynamics of biological molecules. This approach not only deepens our understanding of fundamental biological processes but also paves the way for innovative solutions in various fields, from healthcare to agriculture.

Institute for Energy, Environment and Space (IEES)
The Institute for Energy, Environment, and Space (IEES) is dedicated to accelerating the transition to a sustainable energy system through innovative research, development, and education.
Its Center for Renewable Energy and Energy Efficiency (REEE) works to provide a stronger economy, healthier environment, and sustainable future, empowering communities with clean energy knowledge and practices.
Center for Renewable Energy and Energy Efficiency
The vision of REEE is to accelerate the transition to a sustainable energy system through advanced energy technologies research, development, demonstration and education to provide Egyptians with a stronger economy, healthier environment, and sustainable future.

Center for Aging and Associated Diseases
Helmy Institute for Medical Sciences (HIMS)
Why does a mouse live for two years while an elephant lives for sixty, even though both animals consume the same amount of oxygen during their lifetime? Why do some whales live for 200-300 years while salmon die immediately after they reproduce at two to eight years of age? How do metabolic rates determine the lifespan of all organisms and why are birds a clear exception? Why do some humans age slowly and maintain their cognitive function, while others go through accelerated physical and mental decline while aging? Is it possible to slow down the pace of aging?
These are some of the fundamental questions we are attempting to answer at the Center for Aging and Associated Diseases (CAAD). The center is equipped with state-of-the-art facilities and capitalizes on internationally recognized investigators and brilliant students to explore the mechanisms of aging and degenerative diseases.
The center’s mission is to:
Design and conduct molecular, genetic, cellular, and behavioral research to investigate aging and associated physical and mental disorders.
Study the role of metabolism and oxidative stress in age-associated diseases.
Educate and train cadres of Egyptian scientists and clinicians in the fields of aging and associated chronic and degenerative diseases.
Foster collaborative efforts to enhance clinical, social, and economic research on aging with the primary goal of improving the quality of life of the Egyptian and Arab elderly.
Provide research resources to Egyptian and Arab researchers in the fields of gerontology and geriatrics.
Disseminate information about aging and advances in research to the public, health care professionals, and the scientific community.
The Center for Aging and Associated Diseases is a cutting-edge research facility that is designed to investigate the theories and mechanisms of aging and to identify potential interventional strategies to alleviate age-associated deficits. Our major areas of interest include; Redox Neurobiology, cell metabolism and cancer biology. State-of-the-art techniques – including electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy, confocal imaging microscopy, magnetic resonance imaging, and optical imaging of live cells and animals – are combined with conventional cell and molecular biology studies, genetic manipulations, as well as behavioral studies at CAAD. The center is also equipped with multiple facilities to monitor metabolic pathways in cell and tissue samples.
Examples of our research interests include, but are not limited to, the following research areas:
The roles of mitochondria in aging and degenerative diseases, including the study of mitochondrial respiratory function, signaling under stress, fission and fusion dynamics, interaction with the nucleus, and apoptosis.
Factors leading to the vulnerability of aged populations to cardiovascular and neurodegenerative diseases and intervention strategies to alleviate that vulnerability.
The sources and dynamics of reactive oxygen species in aging organs.
The interrelation between metabolic disorders, such as diabetes and obesity, and aging.
The mechanism of age-associated cognitive and psychological deficits.
Basic muscle biology in metabolic controls and consequences of aging.
Stem cell technologies and regenerative medicine.
Inflammation in aging and diseases.
Longevity studies.

Center for Design and Prototyping
Center for design and prototyping (CDP) is a semi-industrial facility that is being run in research and development environment. CDP serves both industrial and academic communities in which it helps individuals with new ideas and products to develop their initial prototypes, tweak them to enhance quality, functionality and lower their cost to enhance their market competitiveness.
Our work scheme involves reviewing basic principles, developing complete concept design, producing detailed diagrams and manufacturing system components according to international standards. In the later stage, the iterative process of evaluation and enhancement for functionality, material selection, cost reduction is done in close collaboration with the client.
After the evaluation of a successful prototype, based on the client’s request we can help in design and manufacturing of industrial jigs and fixtures for scaling-up, mass production and commercialization.
Our mission is to help the customer to complete the concept design and develop the detailed design and subsequently manufacture the initial prototype and validate it. Our team understands that modifications may be needed, thus the required alterations are made in order to optimize the design for best functionality. If requested, CDP team can work on scaling up and/or design the production processes to help the customer hit the market with a cost-effective product.
Work in CDP is project based where each project is investigated separately and personnel are selected based on required skills to achieve project goals. CDP team supports developing integrated tools and machines that may include mechanical, thermal, fluid, optical, electrical and magnetic components that can be computer controlled.

Center for Genomics
Helmy Institute for Medical Sciences (HIMS)
The Center for Genomics (CG) is a leading research center at Zewail City of Science and Technology (ZC) that employs an interdisciplinary research approach to better understand how genomes function in health and disease. We apply many tools used in genomics research, including physiological, cellular, imaging, molecular, and computational techniques, with the aim of translating genomics research into new methods of treating and diagnosing disease with a tangible impact on society.
A new pillar recently developed in CG is to use high throughput sequencing technologies and analysis pipelines to better understand the integration of microbial infection strategies, host cellular functions, and host defense mechanisms in the pathogenesis of microbial diseases.
CG Facilities
Advance genomic sciences and high impact genomics research in Egypt by providing state-of-the art approaches to:
-Apply interdisciplinary research in molecular and cell biology, molecular genetics, microbial genomics, and computational biology to better understand human health and disease.
-Promote translational research by application and transformation of basic science research into genomic technologies to improve the diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of diseases. For instance, to use genomic data to identify novel targets for therapeutic intervention and to identify candidate loci for genome-guided therapies.
-Provide capacity building in next generation sequencing (NGS) and bioinformatics.
-Provide comprehensive training programs for researchers, students, and professionals in molecular biology, cell biology, molecular genetics, microbials genomics and computational biology.
-Establish collaborative partnerships between academic institutions, industry partners, and end-users across all sectors to empower collaborative genomics research.
Microbial genomics, Molecular biology, Yeast genetics
Key research themes
Investigate DNA repair mechanisms using mammalian cells, yeast and bioinformatics tool
Yeast genetics to discover novel gene functions
Develop a better understanding of the pathogenesis, ecology, evolutionary dynamics, and transmission of zoonotic diseases through the implementation of different population genetics and comparative genomic studies.
Implement bioinformatics pipelines including microbial genome-wide association studies (m-GWAS) to better understand how different mutations recombination events impacted the emergence of different phenotypic traits such as antibiotic resistance, virulence, ecological interactions, and the mechanisms by which pathogens cause disease.

Center for Material Science
NBE Institute for Nanoscience and Informatics (INI)
The Center for Materials Science (CMS) is a multidisciplinary research center that focuses on developing novel materials for applications in demanding technologies, especially those that can contribute to alleviating current problems in the health, energy and water needs of the country. The CMS research scope is broad and involves the design and synthesis of nanomaterials for improved health and environment conditions. The applications include novel smart drug and gene delivery vehicles, nanostructures for tissue engineering, regenerative medicine research and water treatment materials. The development of functional materials for energy and industrial applications focuses on hydrogen storage, gas absorption and separation, heterogeneous catalysis, and solar-to-chemical energy conversions.
CMS aims to have a positive impact at Zewail City across its different centers and with the research community in Egypt at-large through collaborations and the utility of its resources, which include several state-of-the-art research instruments. The main educational mission of the CMS, which is also linked to industrial developments, is involvement in advanced undergraduate and graduate teaching. This is an integral part of the CMS objective.
As part of its outreach mission, the CMS has already initiated collaborations with a number of national and international research institutes and universities. On the local side, for instance, the CMS now has links with the University of Mansoura, Assiut University, Cairo University and Suez Canal University, in addition to Egypt's National Research Center. Other partnership protocols with some international research universities are in progress and these include the University of Texas at Austin and the University of Michigan in the United States.

Ms. Ghada Tawfiq, Governor's Advisor for Corporate Social Responsibility at Central Bank of Egypt, Visit to Zewail City of Science and Technology
December 27, 2023
In the framework of fruitful cooperation between the Central Bank of Egypt and Zewail City of Science, Technology and Innovation, Professor Dr. Mahmoud Abd Rabbo, Acting Executive President of Zewail City, welcomed Ms. Ghada Tawfiq, Governor's Advisor for Corporate Social Responsibility - Central Bank of Egypt.
The visit included a meeting with the innovative young students from the University of Science and Technology in Zewail City, presenting the projects of entrepreneurs from Zewail City graduates, in addition to discussing aspects of future cooperation.
The visit was concluded with a tour inside Zewail City, the Cultural and Administrative Complex, and the new academic building to see and follow all updated developments of the equipped buildings with the latest scientific and laboratory equipment for an integrated student life, and also to follow up on the progress of study and the educational and research facilities in the city.

Zewail City is honored to host Dr. Sultan Al Neyadi
December 26, 2024
On Thursday, the Zewail City of Science and Technology welcomed an Emirati delegation consisting of a group of astronauts from the United Arab Emirates. The delegation was led by His Excellency Dr. Sultan Al Neyadi, UAE Minister of State for Youth Affairs and the astronaut with the longest stay at the International Space Station, which lasted six months, in addition to the first Emirati astronaut, Hazzaa Al Mansoori, along with astronauts Mohammad Al Mulla and Nora Al Matrooshi.
Dr. Mahmoud Abdrabou, Executive President of Zewail City, made sure to welcome the Emirati delegation, highlighting the strong and distinguished relationship between Egypt and the UAE at all levels. He presented an overview of the components of the city and the university, showcasing the significant scientific achievements made so far.
The Emirati astronauts shared the experience of the Mohammed bin Rashid Space Centre and the country's support for this ambitious scientific project, which led to the participation of the first Arab astronaut from the UAE in an eight-day space mission, followed by Dr. Sultan Al Neyadi’s six-month mission.
The UAE Minister of Youth Affairs praised the great scientific renaissance in Egypt, mentioning the distinguished infrastructure he witnessed during his visit to the New Administrative Capital and several Egyptian institutions. He pointed out that the late Nobel laureate Ahmed Zewail was a unique example of the ability of Egyptians and Arabs to achieve scientific dreams and ambitions. He also acknowledged the contributions of Egyptian scientist Dr. Farouk El-Baz to their space endeavors, expressing his hopes for starting scientific cooperation in the field of space between Egypt and the UAE.
Dr. Al Neyadi added that the educational philosophy at Zewail University and its renowned scientific reputation motivated him and his colleagues to visit and meet the university students. He also appreciated the special relationship between Egypt and the UAE on both official and popular levels.
At the end of the visit, : Dr. Mahmoud Abdrabou، on behalf of Dr. Ayman Ashour, Minister of Higher Education and Chairman of the Zewail City Board of Trustees, presented a commemorative shield to Dr. Al Neyadi in recognition of his scientific contributions.

Dorms

- The dorms facility at the University of Science and Technology (UST), Zewail City was inaugurated on September 15th, 2018, with 281 beds and offers accommodation to full-time students, faculty, and staff as well as valued guests of the university.
- The dorms include 133 double rooms and 15 single rooms, all with private bathrooms.
- On March 13th,2021, we received a new building consisting of 93 new rooms, so that the total number of rooms in the student residence now is 241 rooms with a capacity of 467 beds.
- University housing provides housekeeping and cleaning service for all rooms throughout the week except for Friday, along with refrigerators, microwaves, and kettles for each separate floor to serve students and a laundry room for each building.

ZC Clinic
The university clinic provides healthcare services to all members of the university community. Physicians and nurses are available every day from 8:30 a.m. to 4:30 p.m. with the exception of Fridays and Saturdays. Conference and event spaces services
The University of Science and Technology has halls and lounges that are available for organizing events and conferences. The conference and events services office offers assistance to academic and administrative departments for planning university conferences and events. The services include: consultation to plan successful conference registration, equipment provision and cleanup services for outdoor and indoor events.

Dining and food services
A catering and food services company is working on campus under the supervision of the university administration. The company provides a variety of healthy catering services to all faculty, students, researchers and staff. Hot food plates are offered to the whole university community through the cafeteria. The cafeteria also serves refreshments and a variety of hot and cold sandwiches.
The cafeteria is located in the basement of the academic building. Opening hours are from 8:30 a.m. to 6:00 p.m. every day except for Fridays and Saturdays.
The university can offer meal plan contracts to students, faculty and staff for a semester-based fee covering a fixed number of meals per week.

Employability Skills Training:
12 Training Hours focusing on three pillars as follows: Self-Awareness, Soft Skills, and Career Development.
Workshops
Weekly workshops covering topics such as Resume/C.V Writing, Interview Skills, Career Planning, LinkedIn Profile Optimization, HR Foundation, Marketing Basics, Leadership development, and industry-specific skills.
External Trainings and Expert Instructors
Learn from industry professionals, certified trainers and experienced faculty members who bring real-world insights and practical knowledge.
Individual Services
Book your one-to-one meeting and have an individual sessions related to Resume Critique, Mock Interviewing, Job Search, Career Planning and more.
Events
Participate in impactful events and fun activities such as Career Coaching Day, Team Building Day, and more.
For details and further assistance, please contact the Career Advising & Training Services Office
cats@zewailcity.edu.eg

Your Gateway to Career Success
Our Employment Services are dedicated to helping the employer achieve your employer brand and serve as a talent-sourcing hub for some of the best talents in Egypt and supporting our students and alumni to achieve their career aspirations. Whether you are an employer, student, graduate, we offer a range of services to support you.
Career Guidance:
Our students and alumni Receive personalized career advice and guidance and discuss their career goals, job search strategies, and professional development plans.
Access our extensive job board featuring opportunities from top employers across various industries. Find internships, part-time positions, and full-time roles that match your qualifications and interests.
Resume and Cover Letter Assistance:
Get expert feedback on your resume and cover letter to make sure they stand out to potential employers.
Interview Preparation:
Participate in mock interviews and receive constructive feedback to improve your interview skills and confidence.
Employer Partnerships:
Benefit from our strong relationships with leading companies and organizations.
On campus Engagement:
through (real case studies, simulations, competitions, technical trainings, soft-skills development, graduation projects and much more)
Job shadowing / Field trip:
The employer hosts a group of undergraduate students to experience the day-to-day tasks of a professional on his/her job. It helps employers build their presence on campus, while participating in the career exploration process of ZC students helping them make informed career decisions.
Valeo Field Trip on Nov, 15, 2023
Corporate Info sessions:
These sessions are organized on campus to promote career opportunities, summer/winter internships, and publicize info about the employer type of business, culture, and recruitment process.
Dabur Company On-Campus Info Session on Nov, 29, 2023
Internship and job posting:
The employer can post available career vacancies to the ZC students & graduates through the CATS office.
Employment fair:
Employment fairs are organized to give the employers access to highly qualified calibers and the opportunity to showcase their organizations.
For details and further assistance, please contact the Career Advising & Training Services Office
