School Admission
School of Engineering
Programs offered by the School of Engineering (ENGR) emphasize hands-on experience, innovation, soft skills, and multidisciplinary education to help graduates cope with the progressively competitive and dynamic professional environment. Program courses have integrated experimental and/or design elements. All ENGR programs culminate in a two-semester senior design project in which students, working in small groups, demonstrate their mastery of the required engineering competencies and the design process. Furthermore, ENGR programs have courses that tackle complex engineering problems which involve wide-ranging and conflicting technical issues, address problems not encompassed by current standards and codes, involve diverse groups of stakeholders, and include many component parts or sub-problems.
Admission to graduate programs at the School of Engineering is managed by the Office of Admission and Registration (OFAR), the Graduate Program Committees (GPCs) for the different degrees, and the UST Graduate Studies Committee (GSC). The GPCs and GSC are formed by the UST Dean of Academic Affairs and the Executive President of Zewail City.
The diploma and Master of Science (MSc) programs at the UST School of Engineering admit holders of Bachelor of Science (BSc) degrees in engineering disciplines. Holders of BSc degrees in non-engineering disciplines can also apply for diploma and MSc programs at the UST School of Engineering. However, earning these graduate degrees does not entitle students with non-engineering BSc degrees to enroll in the Egyptian Engineers Syndicate.
The PhD programs at the UST School of Engineering admit holders of MSc degrees in engineering disciplines. Holders of MSc degrees who originally have BSc degrees in non-engineering disciplines can also apply for PhD programs at the UST School of Engineering. However, earning a PhD degree from the UST School of Engineering does not entitle students with non-engineering BSc degrees to enroll in the Egyptian Engineers Syndicate.
In all cases, the previous university degree(s) that the applicant has acquired should be in the same or similar field/discipline that the applicant is applying for at the UST School of Engineering. Relevance of the previous university degree(s) is to be assessed by the GPC for the program the applicant is applying for at UST. The assessment by the GPC is subject to the approval of the GSC.
Minimum Admission Requirements:
1. Academic Diploma of Fundamentals of Engineering Sciences
To be granted admission to the UST Academic Diploma of Fundamentals of Engineering Sciences program, an applicant should satisfactorily meet all of the following criteria as minimum requirements for admission:
Hold a BSc with a minimum Cumulative Grade Point Average (cGPA) of 2.5 from an accredited national or international university/ institute.
Pass the admission exam prepared by the GPC for the degree selected by the applicant. Applicants who fail to pass the admission exam may still be admitted to graduate programs provided that they successfully complete qualifying courses as indicated in Section 5.2.2.
Demonstrate proficiency in English as required in Section 5.2.3.
Advanced Diploma in Engineering Sciences
To be granted admission to the UST Advanced Diploma in Engineering Sciences program, an applicant should satisfactorily meet all of the following criteria as minimum requirements for admission:
Hold an Academic Diploma of Fundamentals of Engineering Sciences from an accredited national or international university/ institute. The following additional conditions must be satisfied:
The student has been awarded the Academic Diploma of Fundamentals of Engineering Sciences with a minimum cGPA of 3.0.
The student has been awarded the Academic Diploma of Fundamentals of Engineering Sciences within the last three years from the date of applying for admission to the Advanced Diploma in Engineering Sciences at UST.
The student has obtained the Academic Diploma of Fundamentals of Engineering Sciences in the same (or similar) discipline to which the student is applying for at UST.
Pass the admission exam prepared by the GPC for the degree selected by the applicant. Applicants who fail to pass the admission exam may still be admitted to graduate programs provided that they successfully complete qualifying courses as indicated in Section 5.2.2.
Demonstrate proficiency in English as required in Section 5.2.3. Students who are already enrolled in the Academic Diploma of Fundamentals of Engineering Sciences at the UST School of Engineering may be granted conditional acceptance into the Advanced Diploma in Engineering Sciences in the same discipline provided that these students have successfully completed 75% of the requirements for the Academic Diploma of Fundamentals of Engineering Sciences with a minimum cGPA of 3.0. Students who are granted this conditional acceptance have to complete the remaining requirements for the Academic Diploma of Fundamentals of Engineering Sciences within the maximum duration specified in Section 7.2 and before they can obtain the Advanced Diploma in Engineering Sciences.
3. Master of Science in Engineering
To be granted admission to the UST Master of Science (MSc) in Engineering graduate program, an applicant should satisfactorily meet all of the following criteria as minimum requirements for admission:
Hold a BSc with a minimum cGPA of 2.5 from an accredited national or international university/institute.
Pass the admission exam prepared by the GPC for the degree selected by the applicant. Applicants who fail to pass the admission exam may still be admitted to the MSc program provided that they successfully complete qualifying courses as indicated in Section 5.2.2.
Demonstrate proficiency in English as required in Section 5.2.3. Students applying for an MSc degree at the UST School of Engineering and having already been awarded an Advanced Diploma in Engineering Sciences from an accredited national or international university/ institute may be allowed to register directly for the MSc thesis at UST subject to the following conditions:
The student has been awarded the Advanced Diploma in Engineering Sciences with a minimum cGPA of 3.0 and within the last three years from the date of applying for admission to the MSc program at UST.
The student has obtained the Advanced Diploma in Engineering Sciences in the same discipline to which the student is applying for at UST.
Pass the admission exam prepared by the GPC for the degree selected by the applicant. Applicants who fail to pass the admission exam may still be admitted to the MSc program provided that they successfully complete qualifying courses as indicated in Section 5.2.2.
Demonstrate proficiency in English as required in Section 5.2.3. Students who are already enrolled in the Advanced Diploma in Engineering Sciences at the UST School of Engineering may be granted conditional acceptance into the MSc program in the same discipline provided that these students have successfully completed 75% of the requirements for the Advanced Diploma in Engineering Sciences with a minimum cGPA of 3.0. Students who are granted this conditional acceptance have to complete the remaining requirements for the Academic Diploma of Fundamentals of Engineering Sciences within the maximum duration specified in Section 7.2 and before they can obtain the MSc degree.
4. Doctor of Philosophy in Engineering Sciences
To be granted admission to the Doctor of Philosophy (PhD) graduate program at the UST School of Engineering, an applicant should satisfactorily meet all of the following criteria as minimum requirements for admission:
Hold an MSc degree with a minimum cGPA of 3.0 from a university accredited by SCU. The MSc degree should be in a field/discipline related to the PhD program the student is applying for. Relevance of the MSc degree is to be assessed by the GPC for the PhD degree and is subject to the approval of the GSC.
Pass the admission exam prepared by the GPC for the degree selected by the applicant. Applicants who fail to pass the admission exam may still be admitted to the PhD program provided that they successfully complete qualifying courses as indicated in Section 5.2.2.
Demonstrate proficiency in English as required in Section 5.2.3.
Application for Admission:
Applicants for graduate programs should complete the online application form by the deadline announced on the UST website. Applicants who pass the initial screening are invited to sit for the admission exams prepared by the GPCs for the different degrees. The GPCs then thoroughly review the documents submitted by the applicants who passed the admission exam to select the most qualified candidates. The accepted applicants are notified after final approval by the GSC. The full admission process is illustrated in Figure 3.
1. Supporting Documents
All applicants to graduate programs at the UST School of Engineering must upload the following documents as attachments to their online application forms (Table 1):
Figure 3: Application process for admission to graduate programs at the UST School of Engineering. OFAR is Office of Admission and Registration, GPC is Graduate Program Committee, and GSC is Graduate Studies Committee.
Curriculum Vitae (CV)
Certificates and transcripts of previous university degrees
Written statement of purpose of up to 1000 words highlighting interest in the graduate program, the proposed field of graduate study, and reasons for choosing UST. Applicants for PhD degrees should also attach a research proposal of up to 2000 words. The proposal should outline the research aims and objectives, background, expected contribution, proposed methodology, work plan, resources, and bibliography. Besides the above documents, the applications for MSc and PhD degrees include evaluation forms that ought to be completed by three references. For PhD applicants, the references filling the evaluation forms have to upload recommendation letters with the forms. At least one of the three references should be a faculty member from a university who is familiar with the applicant’s academic performance.
Upon acceptance for admission to graduate programs at UST, students should submit the following official documents:
Original birth certificate
A copy of the National ID for Egyptian applicants
A copy of the passport for non-Egyptian applicants (optional for Egyptian applicants)
Four recent passport–sized photos with white background
General medical report
The original military certificate (for Egyptian males)
Original certificate and original transcript for the BSc degree

After submission of the application, the applicant will receive an e-mail from the UST Office of Admission and Registration confirming the successful submission and assigning a UST ID number to the applicant.
Along with receiving the UST ID number, the applicant will receive instructions to pay the non-refundable application fees.
All applications will be processed, and only short-listed candidates will be selected to sit for the admission exam based on achieving the minimum requirement of the application.
Only short-listed candidates will be invited to take the UST English Proficiency Test if no adequate proof of English proficiency is available as described in Section 5.2.3.
The applicant should keep a copy of confirmation e-mails from the UST Office of Admission and Registration to present at the UST gate along with their national ID Card or passport.
Incomplete applications and applications submitted after the deadline will not be considered
2. Graduate Admission Exam
Upon notification by the Office of Admission and Registration (OFAR), applicants for graduate programs must sit for a UST Graduate Admission Exam. The exam is held by the GPC of the degree selected by the applicant. The exam may be in written and/or oral forms. Applicants who do not pass the exam may still be accepted provided that they pass qualifying courses as per the following regulations:
Qualifying courses are selected by the GPCs.
Qualifying courses should be 400-level courses for students applying for the Academic Diploma of Fundamentals of Engineering Sciences, Advanced Diploma in Engineering Sciences, and for MSc degrees.
Qualifying courses should be 500-level courses for students applying for PhD degrees.
Students have to pass the qualifying courses with a minimum cGPA of 3.0 to proceed to the graduate program that they originally applied for.

Credit Transfer:
Applicants for admission to UST graduate programs who have completed graduate courses at a nationally or internationally accredited university other than UST may request an evaluation for transfer of academic credit hours.
External non-UST courses may be considered for transfer credit if all criteria for assessment are met.
Transferred courses can be used to satisfy course requirements for graduate programs at the UST School of Engineering.
The external courses must first be submitted for review by the UST Office of Admission and Registration. After the external courses have been reviewed and deemed transferable by the Office of Admission and Registration, the relevant GPC determines if each of the reviewed transferable courses can be used to satisfy the degree requirement based on a comparison between the complete non-UST course syllabus to be transferred and the UST course syllabus.
The transfer regulations are as follows:
For courses completed outside of the UST School of Engineering, the maximum Credit Hours that may be counted toward a graduate degree at the UST School of Engineering are as follows:
3 Credit Hours for Academic Diploma of Fundamentals of Engineering Sciences
3 Credit Hours for Advanced Diploma in Engineering Sciences
6 Credit Hours for an MSc Degree split as follows:
3 Credit Hours for MSc courses that count toward completing an Academic Diploma of Fundamentals of Engineering Sciences
3 Credit Hours for MSc courses that count toward completing an Advanced Diploma in Engineering Sciences
6 Credit Hours for a PhD Degree
All requests for transfer credit and official transcripts should be submitted no later than six weeks before the start of the semester to be enrolled in.
To be considered as transfer student, an applicant must be in a good academic standing at the previously enrolled institution. The applicant must have a Cumulative Grade Point Average (cGPA) of at least 3.00 to be considered for application.
The external courses to be transferred have been completed at a nationally accredited institution in Egypt or at an officially recognized institution in a foreign country (recognized by SCU or ABET accredited).
The external courses to be transferred are substantially similar to UST courses and no more than three years have passed since the courses were completed.
The final grade for each potential transfer course should not be less than a ’
’ grade as assigned by the institution from which the student is being transferred.
Partial course transfer is not accepted; only full transfer satisfying the criteria set in this section is allowed.
The university offering the courses allows these courses to be used for credit towards its own graduate degrees.
Transcripts from the nationally or internationally accredited institution must be sent directly to the UST School of Engineering. Transcripts cannot be accepted directly from the student.
The transcript should clearly indicate all of the following information for each course:
Course code or number
Course title and description
Final grade earned
Course credits earned
Course Syllabus
The relevant GPC conducts the evaluation and reports the recommendations to the Graduate Studies Committee (GSC).
The GSC makes the final decision regarding the recommended transfer credits.

School Admission
School of Science
The UST School of Science currently offers Bachelor of Science degree programs in the fields of biomedical sciences, nano science, and physics of universe.
Programs offered by the UST School of Science (SCI) provide graduates with an ability to identify, formulate, and solve technical and scientific problems; develop and conduct experiments and test hypotheses; analyze and interpret data; and assess the impact of technical and scientific solutions in global, economic, environmental, and societal contexts. UST SCI programs emphasize experiential
learning, effective communication, collaborative work, management skills, and multidisciplinarity. All UST SCI programs culminate in a capstone project in which students demonstrate their ability to formulate a system, process, procedure, or program to meet desired needs.
Admission Criteria For MSc Degrees:
To be granted admission to the UST M.Sc. graduate program, an applicant should satisfactorily meet all the following criteria as minimum requirements for admission:
B.Sc. from an accredited national or international university/institute, with a minimum grade of 2.5. The Program Curriculum Committee (GPC) can raise the minimum grade up to 3.0.
A B.Sc. in Science with specialization in Mathematics is required for admission to the M.Sc. program in Applied Mathematics.
The original certificate and the original transcript of the B.Sc. should be submitted. If the B.Sc. is from an international university/institute, the equivalency by the Supreme Council of Universities is required.
Applicants must provide proof of English language proficiency.
UST accepts any of the following English tests:
TOEFL iBT
IELTS

Completed online application form. Motivation letter, letters of recommendation, and copies of certificates and transcripts of previous university degrees should be attached with the application.
Submission of the following applicant documents:
Curriculum Vitae (CV),
Original Birth Certificate,
A copy of the National ID for the Egyptian applicants,

The program committee applies “Equal Opportunity Selection” to all applicants regardless of color, gender, religion, disability, or nationality
Due to limited capacity and high caliber of applicants, meeting the minimum requirements might not grant admission into the M.Sc. Graduate Program. The most qualified applicants will be selected.
PhD Admission Criteria
To be granted admission to the Ph.D. graduate program, an applicant should satisfactorily meet all the following criteria as minimum requirements for admission:
Holding an M.Sc. degree in the field or close to the field of the discipline the candidate is applying for, from an accredited institute by the SCU. (The Dean of Academic Affairs and the discipline’s GPC will decide if the M.Sc. degree is close to the applied discipline).
The original certificate and the original transcript of the B.Sc. should be submitted. If the B.Sc. is from an international university/institute, the equivalency by the Supreme Council of Universities is required.
The original certificate and the original transcript of the M.Sc. should be submitted. If the M.Sc. is from an international university/institute, the equivalency by the Supreme Council of Universities is required.
For applicants to the Ph.D. in Physics program, passing the GRE Subject Test in Physics with a minimum score of 700
Submission of three reference letters, two of which are obtained from professor’s familiar with the candidate’s work/academic performance. Internal supervisor, from Zewail City, may support the candidate with one of the three reference letters. If the applicant is a Zewail City graduate, the three letters may be from internal Zewail City faculty.
Submission of the following applicant documents:
Curriculum Vitae (CV),
Original Birth Certificate,
A copy of the National ID for the Egyptian applicants,
A copy of the passport for non-Egyptian applicants (optional for Egyptian applicants),
Four personal photos,
General medical report, and
The original military certificate (for Egyptian males).
Submission of a written statement of purpose composed of a maximum of 1000 words highlighting interest in the graduate program, the proposed field of graduate study, and reasons for choosing University of Science and Technology at Zewail City.
The candidate
must pass the “CANDIDACY EXAM”.
The Ph.D. Candidacy Exam is a written comprehensive exam in the discipline of the Ph.D. It covers the contents in the compulsory courses of the program requirements in the M.Sc. study plan.
The applicant has only two trials to pass the
CANDIDACY EXAM,
which may be offered twice a year (exam time might differ as announced by the program).
The candidate will be formally accepted as a Ph.D. candidate after passing the candidacy examination and approving his/her doctoral plan.
Due to limited capacity and competitive competency, meeting the minimum requirements might not grant admission into the Ph.D. Graduate Program. The most qualified applicants will be selected.
Submission of online application form. Recommendation letters, personal statement, research proposal, and certificates and transcripts of previous university degrees should be attached with the application by the announced deadline.
Selection process:
All applications are processed and assessed by the graduate program committee (GPC) and approved by the graduate studies committee (GSC).
The program committee applies “Equal Opportunity Selection” to all applicants regardless of colour, gender, religion, disability or nationality.
Short-listed applicants who passed the initial screening phase will be interviewed by a selection committee, where he/she will have to present a research topic and discuss it with the committee.

DEFINITIONS
A Program represents the area of study a student has chosen to pursue for the bachelor’s degree, which
consists of a specified common group of courses in the discipline(s) covered by the Program in the School
of Business.
A Degree is conferred by the university after the student satisfactorily completes the required number of
credit hours with at least the minimum required cumulative GPA as specified in this handbook.
A Credit Hour is the measuring unit for educational credit a student receives for successfully completing
a course. The total credit hours for a course depends on the number of classroom hours per week spent in
the course.

3. ACTUARIAL ANALYSIS AND RISK MANAGEMENT (AARM) – 130 CR
3.1. PROGRAM DESCRIPTION
The Actuarial Analysis and Risk Management (AARM) Program enables graduates to understand the
process of how to identify, measure, and manage risk as well as understand how to mitigate and manage
finance loss exposures using alternative risk management techniques. Additionally, graduates will be able
to analyze and design insurance products and corporate risk management strategies. They will be able to
put together a framework for decision making for the operation of insurance and related financial service
sector firms. Graduates will also be able to demonstrate skill producing quality independent research using
advanced statistical and simulation tools.

3.2. PROGRAM EDUCATIONAL OBJECTIVES (PEOS)
The AARM Program nurtures an academic environment that contributes effectively to empower its
graduates towards having the choice, talents, and knowledge to transition into a broad range of career
options.
Within four years of graduation, the AARM Program graduates should be able to:
1. Understand how to analyze and evaluate business processes combined with a capability for
improving those processes via a theoretical foundation and quantitative skills, alongside
practical problem-solving techniques
2. Demonstrate the ability to apply actuarial concepts to problems related to financial security
planning such as pension funding, pricing and reserving for life and casualty insurance
3. Practice as professional risk analysts who provide solutions for financial and business
problems involving uncertain future events
4. Prepare for a career in consultancy, finance, retail, manufacturing, enterprise analytics units,
IT systems, outsourcing and telecoms.
5. Analyze and implement operational business decisions from both strategic and tactical
perspectives
6. Pursue advanced degrees in local and international graduate programs in actuarial sciences
and risk management or related fields to emerge as thought leaders, researchers, experts, and
educators.
7. Remain current in their chosen careers through being lifelong learners in ever-changing global
economic environments.

04010000000002_202301_20260120_01
3.3. STUDENT OUTCOMES (SOS)
Student Outcomes describe what students are expected to be able to do by the time of graduation.
Graduates of the AARM program will have the ability to:
1. Analyze complex actuarial analysis and risk management problems and apply principles of analytics
and other relevant disciplines to identify solutions.
2. Design, implement and evaluate analytics and business-based solutions to meet a given set of risk
management requirements in the context of the program’s discipline.
3. Communicate effectively in a variety of professional contexts.
4. Recognize professional responsibilities and make informed judgments in actuarial and risk
management practice based on legal and ethical principles.
5. Function effectively as a member or leader of a team engaged in activities appropriate to the
program’s discipline.

4. FINANCE AND INVESTMENT MANAGEMENT (FIM) – 130 CR
4.1. PROGRAM DESCRIPTION
The Finance and Investment Management (FIM) Program focuses on professional financial services
practice. The curriculum is designed to prepare students to analyze complex financial structures and
design optimized investment strategies. The program prepares graduates to build and leverage their
background in analytics to inform financial decision making for various sectors.
برنامج النظم المالية
وإدارة
االستثمار
يركز برنامج التمويل وإدارة االستثمار على ممارسة الخدمات المالية المهنية .
تم تصميم المنهج إلعداد الطالب لتحليل الهياكل المالية المعقدة وتصميم
استراتيجيات االستثمار األمثل .

القطاعات .
4.2. PROGRAM EDUCATIONAL OBJECTIVES (PEOS)
The FIM Program nurtures an academic environment that contributes effectively to empower its
graduates towards having the skills, talents, and knowledge to attain the following educational objectives
within four years of graduation to:
1. Explain the trade-off between risk and returns, explain methods of measuring and managing risk,
and use financial models, including those for optimal portfolios and estimation of expected
returns.
2. Distinguish between equilibrium and no-arbitrage pricing and be able to apply both approaches
and explain the costs and benefits of the separation of ownership and control in the typical large
firm.
3. Understand how market frictions can influence financial decisions and explain how investment
and financing decisions can create and destroy value.
4. Pursue a successful career in finance and investment management and contribute to the
advancement of businesses through investment management.
5. Pursue advanced degrees in local and international graduate programs in finance and
investment management and related fields to emerge as thought leaders, researchers, experts,
and educators.
6. Be a lifelong learner in the field of Finance and Investment Management and remain current in
the emerging markets and methodologies in their career. And assume a leadership role in the
field of Finance and Investment Management.

04010000000002_202301_20260120_01
4.3. STUDENT OUTCOMES (SOS)
Student Outcomes describe what students are expected to be able to do by the time of graduation.
Graduates of the FIM Program will have the ability to:
1. Maximize profits by providing insights on, for example, rising costs of raw materials that might
trigger an increase in the cost of goods sold.
2. Track liquidity and cash flow to ensure the company has enough money on hand to meet its
obligations.
3. Ensure compliance with national and industry-specific regulations.
4. Develop financial scenarios based on business current state and forecasts that assume a wide range
of outcomes based on possible market conditions.
5. Deal effectively with investors and the boards of directors.
6. Function effectively as a member or leader of a team engaged in activities appropriate to the
program’s discipline.
7. Apply theory, techniques, and tools throughout the data analysis lifecycle and employ the resulting
knowledge to satisfy stakeholders’ needs.

5. MARKETING, ENTREPRENEURSHIP, AND INNOVATION MANAGEMENT (MEIM) – 130 CR
5.1. PROGRAM DESCRIPTION
The Marketing, Entrepreneurship, and Innovation Management (MEIM) Program instills its graduates with
the concepts, skills, and applications necessary to design and operate successful startup businesses. The
Program focuses on allowing goals to organically develop, along with tactics like experiential marketing
and bootstrapping. Focusing on innovation and entrepreneurship will provide undergraduate students an
opportunity to explore new organizational types – profit and non-profit, startups, as well as new and
unique solutions to problems and develop necessary skill sets to become an effective entrepreneur as well
as an innovative thinker. Students examine current situations and offer innovative solutions by employing
creativity, innovation, and an entrepreneurial focus.

5.2. PROGRAM EDUCATIONAL OBJECTIVES (PEOS)
Within four years of graduation, the MEIM Program graduates should be able to:
1. Understand the significance and contribution of marketing to the business enterprise.
2. Define primary and secondary sources of information; give examples of methods used to collect
primary data; give examples of sources for secondary data; and compare and contrast the
advantages and disadvantages of both types of data.
3. Describe major bases for segmenting consumer and business markets; define and be able to
apply the three steps of target marketing: market segmentation, target marketing and market
positioning; understand how different situations in the competitive environment will affect
choices in target marketing.
4. Describe the major types of consumer buying behavior, the stages in the buyer decision process
and completely outline the components of the marketing mix; identify how the firms marketing
strategy and marketing mix must evolve and adapt to match consumer behavior and perceptions
of the product (e.g., classification of products and services, brand image, price and value), the
stage in the product life cycle and the competitive environment; summarize the importance of
measuring and managing return on marketing.
5. List and describe the steps in the new-product development (NPD) process; describe how the
NPD process meshes with the adoption and diffusion process for those products.
6. Apply best practices in the development and use of information systems to solve real-world
problems, enable organizations to meet the opportunities and challenges of a digital economy,
and improve the lives of people, communities, and societies.
7. Actively contribute to the profession through service, life-long learning, and support of
diversity and inclusion in their organizations.

04010000000002_202301_20260120_01
5.3. STUDENT OUTCOMES (SOS)
Student Outcomes describe what students are expected to be able to do by the time of graduation.
Graduates of the MEIM program will have the ability to:
1. Identify entrepreneurial and innovative opportunities for different organizational structures; i.e.,
business, government, or social organizations.
2. Design a conceptual and a functional business plan for today’s global economy. Additionally, apply
relevant technology and research information for competitive advantage in an entrepreneurial and
innovative environment.
3. Demonstrate understanding of entrepreneurism in diverse organizational contexts, including startups, small and mid-sized businesses, and corporate ventures.
4. Demonstrate understanding of business law and ethical decision-making as it relates to the
business, employees, and stakeholders.
5. Support the delivery, use, and management of information systems within an information systems
environment.
6. Recognize professional responsibilities and make informed judgments in computing practice based
on legal and ethical principles.

6. OPERATIONS, SUPPLY CHAIN, AND TECHNOLOGY MANAGEMENT (OSCTM) – 130 CR
6.1. PROGRAM DESCRIPTION
The Operations, Supply Chain, and Technology Management (OSCTM) Program instills its graduates with
the concepts, skills, and applications necessary to design and operate technology driven supply chain and
operations for enterprises. The program provides a sound education in business with an emphasis on
operations, including supply chain management, project management, quantitative methods, and quality
control. OSCTM graduates understand how to be effective as managers and leaders and are equipped to
make strategic contributions through knowledge of operations, supply chain, logistics, production, quality,
project management, transportation, and technology.

6.2. PROGRAM EDUCATIONAL OBJECTIVES (PEOS)
Within four years of graduation, the OSCTM Program graduates should be able to:
1. Create business value through the analysis of appropriate data using statistical and/or
optimization techniques benefiting sourcing, inventory and supply chain management.
2. Assess the financial, marketing, and operational implications of fundamental supply chain
strategies, and compare how supply chain strategies are applied by examining decisions
made in various real-world settings. Evaluate opportunities and risks necessary to develop
effective sourcing strategies.
3. Apply technical and management principles in a technical environment to achieve
operational excellence in management of innovation.
4. Apply technical skills such as quality, analysis, project management, design methods, and
other necessary specialties in the field of technology management.
5. Synthesize operation management concepts into a business strategy that is implemented
through a cross-functional business administration.
6. Demonstrate strong critical thinking skills as observed through their ability to debate
various positions, ask skeptical questions, and probe underlying assumptions
7. Demonstrate leadership qualities in moving the profession forward. Effectively
communicate ideas and recommendations to individuals in all functional areas of an
organization.
8. Apply best practices in the development and use of information systems to solve real-world
problems, enable organizations to meet the opportunities and challenges of a digital
economy, and improve the lives of people, communities, and societies

04010000000002_202301_20260120_01
6.3. STUDENT OUTCOMES (SOS)
Student Outcomes describe what students are expected to be able to do by the time of graduation.
Graduates of the OSCTM program will have the ability to:
1. Analyze a complex computing problem and to apply principles of computing and other relevant
disciplines to identify solutions.
2. Design, implement, and evaluate a computing-based solution to meet a given set of computing
requirements in the context of the program’s discipline.
3. Communicate effectively in a variety of professional contexts.
4. Function effectively as a member or leader of a team engaged in activities appropriate to the
program’s discipline.
5. Support the delivery, use, and management of information systems within an information systems
environment.
6. Recognize professional responsibilities and make informed judgments in computing practice based
on legal and ethical principles.

6.5. DEGREE REQUIREMENTS
A Student who intends to major in OSCTM must submit a Program Declaration Form before the first
semester. Students should consult their academic advisor on a regular basis to ensure the prerequisites
for their University Requirements (21 Cr), School Requirements (70 Cr), and the following program
requirements are fulfilled.
The program provides specialization in operations, supply chain and technology management, which
requires successful completion of 39 credit hours consisting of the following compulsory courses:

Definitions
• A Credit Hour is the measuring unit for educational credit a student receives for successfully
completing a course. The total credit hours for a course depends on the number of classroom
hours per week spent in the course.
• A Program represents the area of study a student has chosen to pursue for the undergraduate
degree.
• A Major is a part of a Program which consists of a specified common group of courses in the
discipline(s) covered by the Program.
• Concentration is an alternative track of courses within a Program.
• A Minor consists of a specified group of courses in a particular discipline or field that a student
optionally chooses outside the Program.
• A Grade Point Average (GPA) for a student is a measure of the student’s academic
achievement. The GPA is defined by a number on a scale of 0 to 4.0, representing the weighted
average of the accumulated final grades earned in courses within the student’s program.
• Cumulative GPA (CGPA) refers to the overall GPA accumulated at the time of GPA calculation.
• Semester GPA is calculated for a single semester.
• A Degree is conferred by the university after the student satisfactorily completes the required
number of credit hours with at least the minimum required CGPA as specified in this handbook.

Studying at the University of Science and Technology
The University of Science and Technology (UST) is an integral part of Zewail City of Science,
Technology and Innovation (ZC). UST is the hub connecting academic excellence in cutting-edge
research at the research centers and the industry through the Valley of Science and Technology.
UST invests in the younger generations through providing the optimal environment for learning and
development.
The curricula for UST programs are carefully designed to prepare graduates to take leadership roles in
the innovation age and equip them with the necessary knowledge and skills to face the challenges
in the region including endemic diseases, the need for improved health care systems, environmental
management, water treatment, utilization of renewable energy resources, and many other challenges.
UST graduates are expected to acquire a deep understanding of basic sciences as well as handson experience with cutting-edge technologies. The programs and concentrations offered at UST
expose the students to research facilities and laboratories enabling them to conduct both basic
scientific research and industry-linked research projects. The interdisciplinary nature of UST programs
provides ample opportunities for sharing knowledge and creative thinking among its students. This
interdisciplinarity is essential to enable UST graduates to develop novel, non-conventional solutions
for problems that have significant social, economic, and environmental impacts.
UST graduates can choose to continue their academic careers and become actively involved in research
and teaching. Many UST graduates choose to take the skills they acquired at UST into employment
in business and industry, in both technical and non-technical jobs. Other UST graduates choose to
combine their technical skills with entrepreneurial roles in starting new businesses.

Mission of the University of Science and Technology
The mission of the University of Science and Technology (UST) is to serve the people of Egypt
through pre-eminence in creating, communicating, and applying knowledge, through science, research,
technology, and academic values. UST strives to develop leaders and citizens who challenge the
present and enrich the future.
UST prepares students to embark on 21st century knowledge society guided by our policy: "Admission
to the university is only based on merit".
The University is defined by a culture of interdisciplinary teaching and research, coupled with academic
rigor. By transcending disciplinary boundaries, we encourage our students, faculty, and staff to tackle
complex and vexing challenges facing modern societies at local, national, and global levels.
Our programs are responsive to the changing needs of society and relevant to the goals of our students
and the needs of the community. Our academic environment is rich in opportunities for independence
and collaboration and reflective of the traditions of excellence, innovation, and leadership that the
University aims to establish.
This mission is achieved by:
• Building a strong foundation in sciences and engineering.
• Providing the knowledge and skills essential for career and personal success.
• Integrating teaching, research, and service in ways that enhance the learning experience.
• Supporting a dynamic environment where innovation, openness, and creativity are fostered.
• Using advanced technologies to meet the changing educational needs and to establish links
with the global community.
• Establishing partnerships with business, industry, educational institutions, and government
agencies.

Structure of the University of Science and Technology
UST currently includes four schools: the School of Science (SCI), the School of Engineering (ENGR),
the School of Computational Sciences and Artificial Intelligence (CSAI), and the School of Business
(BUS) (Figure 1). The School of Science and the School of Engineering have been offering academic
programs since 2013. The School of Computational Sciences and Artificial Intelligence and the School
of Business have started offering their academic programs in 2022 and 2023, respectively. Besides the
four schools, UST includes a General Education Department that offers English language, humanities,
and social sciences courses to all UST schools and programs.

The UST School of Business
The UST School of Business (BUS) stands as a distinguished institution within Zewail City, rooted in
the visionary ideals of its founder, the late Dr. Ahmed Zewail. As the newest major pillar of Zewail
City’s mission to establish a sustainable knowledge society in Egypt, BUS is committed to shaping
the future of business education and fostering transformative leadership.
BUS offers a comprehensive range of Bachelor of Business Administration degree programs that
exemplify excellence in education and reflect the evolving needs of the global business landscape.
Our programs include actuarial analysis and risk management; finance and investment management;
marketing, entrepreneurship, and innovation management; as well as operations, supply chain, and
technology management (Figure 2). Through rigorous curriculum, innovative teaching methodologies,
and experiential learning opportunities, we prepare our students to thrive in dynamic and complex
business environments.
Aligned with Dr. Ahmed Zewail’s vision, BUS serves as a catalyst for societal progress, emphasizing
sustainable practices and responsible leadership. By nurturing a deep understanding of the interplay
between business and the environment, we aim to equip our graduates with the knowledge and skills
to drive positive change, ensuring a more equitable and prosperous future for all.
As an integral part of Zewail City, BUS draws upon the institution’s rich heritage of research-led
education in Engineering and Science Programs. By fostering interdisciplinary collaboration and
leveraging Zewail City’s state-of-the-art facilities and resources, BUS offers a unique advantage. Our
students benefit from a holistic educational experience, combining business acumen with scientific
and technological expertise, positioning them as versatile professionals equipped to tackle emerging
challenges in an increasingly interconnected world.
Committed to excellence, BUS distinguishes itself from other regional business schools through its
rigorous academic standards, innovative curriculum, and emphasis on research-driven education.
Our faculty comprises accomplished scholars and industry practitioners, ensuring the delivery of
cutting-edge knowledge and practical insights. Moreover, our commitment to sustainability, ethical
business practices, and societal impact sets us apart as a beacon of responsible business education in
the region.
The School of Business at Zewail City embodies visionary leadership, interdisciplinary collaboration,
and a commitment to sustainability converge. With this addition to our academic offerings at Zewail
City, we forge a path towards creating future business leaders who will drive positive change, shape
industries, and contribute to the advancement of the Egyptian knowledge society on both local and
global scales.

Application Procedures and Evaluation
Note: For the application procedures and evaluation applicable to the academic year 2023–2024, refer
to Appendix A.
Basic Requirements to Apply
• The UST School of Business accepts applicants from the Egyptian Secondary School General
Certificate (Thanaweya Amma) or equivalent certificates approved by the Supreme Council of
Universities (SCU) - according to the validity period set by SCU for the certificates.
• The minimum required score for applying is announced by UST on annual basis when admissions
are opened.
Initial Evaluation
• Interested candidates should complete the UST online initial evaluation form to ensure that
they meet the basic requirements set forth by the UST Office of Admission and Registration.
• This process requires information about the type of high school degree, scores in specific subjects,
the overall grade/score at graduation, graduation year, program, and related information.
Online Application
• Based on the results of the initial evaluation, eligible applicants are directed to proceed with
filling the online application form.
• Applicants must upload the following documents as attachments to their application:
a. High School Transcripts or Statements of Results.
b. Birth certificate, national ID, or a passport copy.
c. Proof of English proficiency if available: Test scores of IELTS (Academic), TOEFL (IBT),
or Pearson PTE (Academic).
d. Passport–size photo with a white background.
• After submission of the application, the applicant will receive an e-mail from the UST Office of
Admission and Registration confirming the successful submission and assigning an applicant ID
number.
• Along with receiving the applicant ID, the applicant will receive instructions to pay the nonrefundable application fees.
• All applications will be processed, and only shortlisted candidates achieving the minimum
requirements will be contacted to complete the admission process.
• The applicant should keep a copy of confirmation e-mails from the UST Office of Admission
and Registration to present at the UST gate along with their national ID Card or passport.
• Incomplete applications and applications submitted beyond the deadline will not be considered.

• After being admitted to UST, new students are required to sit for an English Placement Test or
submit results of standardized English tests to evaluate the students’ English proficiency and
determine if remedial English courses are needed (see Section A.4).
Required Documents
Upon acceptance for admission to UST, students should submit the following official documents:
• Original birth certificate.
• Four recent passport–sized photos with white background.
• 2 copies of national ID for Egyptian students.
• Military form for all male students.
• Copy of passport for non-Egyptian students.
• Medical certificate and HIV certificate for foreigners.
• High school certificate as follows:
– Holders of the Egyptian secondary school (Thanaweya Amma) Certificate should submit
their original certificate.
– Applicants with non-Egyptian secondary school certificates must have an equivalent high
school certificate approved by the Supreme Council of Universities in Egypt (SCU). The
high school certificate should be endorsed by the authorities concerned in the country
issuing it, the Egyptian Embassy, and the Egyptian Ministry of Foreign Affairs and approved
by the Public Administration for Examinations (Ministry of Education).
• High school certificate holders from outside Egypt must provide a proof of residence abroad.
The proof must be authenticated by the Egyptian Cultural Counsellor or the Egyptian Embassy.
An alternative would be to present the student’s passport indicating the entry and exit dates
during the study period or to present an official entry/exit travel record.
• Original document of Arabic Language and Religion exams Thanaweya Amma level for foreign
certificate holders, if available.
Transfer Criteria
5.5.1
Transfer to UST
Undergraduates who are applying for admission to UST and have completed courses at a nationally or
internationally accredited university other than UST may request an evaluation for transfer of credit
hours.
External non-UST courses may be considered for transfer credit if all criteria for assessment are met.
Transferred courses can be used to satisfy UST University requirements, School requirements, and/or
Program requirements.
The transfer regulations are as follows:

04010000000001_202301_20250816_01

• No more than 32 Credit Hours of external non-UST courses may be counted toward an
undergraduate degree at the UST School of Business.
• All requests for transfer credit and official transcripts should be submitted to the UST Office
of Admission and Registration no later than six weeks before the start of the semester to be
enrolled in.
• To be considered as transfer student, an applicant must be in a good academic standing at the
previously enrolled institution. The applicant must have a Cumulative Grade Point Average
(CGPA) of at least 3.0000 or a minimum accumulative general grade of Very Good to be
considered for application.
• The certificate for secondary school completion will be considered as a main factor in the
admission process and the applicant must meet the minimum high school requirement announced
at the time the applicant has completed the secondary school.
• The student should pass the interview and any required admission tests.
• The external courses to be transferred have been completed at a nationally accredited institution
in Egypt or at an officially recognized institution in a foreign country (recognized by SCU or
accredited).
• The external courses to be transferred are substantially similar to UST courses and no more
than five years have passed since the courses were completed.
• The final grade for each potential transfer course should not be less than a B grade or ”Very
Good” as assigned by the institution from which the student is being transferred.
• Partial course transfer is not accepted; only full transfer satisfying the criteria set in this section
is allowed.
• The university offering the courses allows these courses to be used for credit towards its own
undergraduate degree.
• Transcripts from the nationally or internationally accredited institution must be sent directly to
the UST Office of Admission and Registration. Transcripts cannot be accepted directly from
the student. The transcript should clearly indicate all of the following information for each
course:
a. Course code or number
b. Course title and description
c. Final grade earned
d. Course credits earned
e. Course Syllabus
• After the external courses have been screened and deemed transferable by the Office of Admission
and Registration, the Dean of Academic Affairs reviews the screened transferable courses.
• Based on the review by the Dean, transferable courses are evaluated by the Curriculum
Committee of the Program the student is applying for.

• Recommendations from the Program Curriculum Committee are reported to the Dean of
Academic Affairs to make the final decision.
5.5.2
Transfer between UST Schools
• Undergraduate students who are enrolled in a UST school may apply for transfer to a different
UST school.
• Students should consult with their academic advisor before submitting a school transfer
application and paying the processing fee.
• Students who apply to transfer to another UST school should pay a processing fee for their
transfer application to be considered.
• For approval of their transfer application, the students’ must satisfy admission requirements for
the UST school they want to transfer to. The applicable admission requirements correspond
to the students’ original date of admission to UST and not the date of the school transfer
application.
• Approval of the transfer application is subject to the availability of resources and capacity at
the UST school the student is applying to transfer to.
• The academic records of students who are allowed to transfer to a different UST school will
continue to include all courses studied since the initial admission to UST.

Tuition and Scholarships
Tuition
• Studying at UST is based on the Credit Hours system, in which a Credit Hour is the measuring
unit for the student workload.
• Students enrolled at the UST School of Business are required to complete 130 Credit Hours to
graduate as indicated in Section 8.9.
• All remedial English courses registered by the students are added to the required tuition fees.
An English Remedial course cost is equivalent to 2 Credit Hours. The repeat policy applies to
the remedial English courses.
• Tuition is calculated based on the number of registered credit hours in each academic semester.
• The tuition fees include access to mandatory laboratory supplies for experiments, library, scientific
databases, and access to career advising services.
• The tuition fee per credit hour is subject to an annual increase for the newly admitted students
and for the returning students1.
• The tuition fees are posted on the ZC website by the start of each Academic Year.
Scholarships
• UST awards a range of merit based scholarships.
• Scholarships only cover tuition for the original degree program the student was admitted to.
• Scholarships do not cover repeats, remedial courses, minors, double majors, or anything outside
the standard program.
• Details about types and conditions for scholarships are posted on the ZC website.
• The announced scholarship scheme is subject to change for newly admitted students.
• The status of scholarship recipients is re-evaluated by the end of the Summer Semester of every
Academic Year.
Payment of Tuition Fees
6.3.1
Procedures and Instructions
• After the final acceptance of joining UST, newly-admitted students must immediately pay a
non-refundable deposit.
• The amount of the non-refundable deposit is posted on the ZC website by the start of each
Academic Year.
• Online registration should be completed by the deadline mentioned in the UST calendar.
1For students admitted before Fall 2024, the annual tuition fee increase does not apply to returning students; it
applies only to newly admitted students.

• All non-Egyptian students are required to pay their tuition fees in United States dollars (USD).
• If the student believes that any of the fee information displayed is incorrect, he/she should
contact the Student Accounts & Financial Support Office.
6.3.2
Tuition Fees Payment Period
• The payment process of the university tuition fees is allowed only during the announced
Registration period (see section 8.8).
• The tuition fees may be paid through instalments as per each student’s payment plan by
submitting the original payment receipt to the Student Accounts Office each semester.
• Students who are having their tuition fees paid through a “third party” are responsible for
finalizing the payment prior to the deadline, otherwise their added courses will be dropped, and
they will not be permitted to attend the course sessions.
• Students are responsible to make sure that their payment is finalized before the deadline of
payment.
6.3.3
Deferred Payment
• With the permission of the Office of the Admission and Registration/ Office of Financial Affairs,
students may be allowed to defer payment of up to 50% of their tuition fees.
6.3.4
Late Fee
• For students who do not pay the due amounts by the announced deadlines, they will be required
to pay a late fee as specified by the Board of Directors. The late fee will be due at the time of
paying the overdue tuition fees.
Tuition Refunds
• In case of withdrawal from UST, the student is entitled to a full or partial refund of tuition fees
according to the policy given in Table 1.
• Refund Percentage is deducted from the full tuition.
• The refund policy is applied to the returning and new students. In addition, for new students,
the initial non-refundable deposit will not be refunded.

Rights and Responsibilities of Students
Privacy Rights of Students
• UST has adopted policies and procedures to protect the privacy rights of students.
• UST provides access to student records and files only to those employees who have justification
for using the student records connected with the employee’s university responsibilities.
• A written consent has to be received from the student before any restricted data is released
from the student’s record to any party except those authorized by UST. Disclosure may also
be made to other persons or organizations under certain conditions such as accreditation or
university evaluation or other university responsibilities.
• Consent is not required where the disclosure is to UST officials who have a legitimate educational
interest.
Students’ Rights within the University Community
UST affirms the following students’ rights and privileges:
• The right to pursue academic goals without being subject to discrimination based on race, color,
religion, ethnicity, geographic origin, age, sex, marital status, or handicapped status.
• Freedom to join organizations, speak freely, engage in discussion, make inquiries, exchange
thought and opinion, publish and exchange findings and recommendations, speak, write, or
print freely on any subject, and sponsor speakers of their choice, subject only to the right of
the University to make reasonable rules and regulations related thereto.
• To associate with whomsoever they please.
• To engage in the educational process.
• To be secure in their persons, living quarters, papers, and effects from unreasonable, illegal, or
unauthorized searches and seizures.

Academic Policies and Regulations
• The following general academic rules and regulations apply to undergraduate students at UST
to govern the relationship between the University and the students. Therefore, all students are
urged to read these regulations and are expected to comply with them.
• UST reserves the right to make amendments to these regulations. Students should consult the
Dean of Strategic Enrollment Management for further inquiries.
• Each program may have additional specific requirements over and above the following regulations.
English Placement Test
• English is the language of study at UST.
• Immediately after being granted admission to UST, all new students are required to demonstrate
English language proficiency by taking the UST English Placement Test (EPT) which is a
computer-based exam that consists of the following four sections:
a. Listening
b. Reading
c. Writing
d. Speaking
• As an alternative to sitting for the UST EPT, newly admitted students can provide the results
of the following English tests:
– TOEFL iBT
– IELTS
– Pearson PTE (Academic)
• Applicants may be required to take one or more remedial English courses depending on the
applicants’ scores in the English test. Details about requirements for remedial English courses
are posted on the ZC website.
Declaring Program
• Students applying for admission to UST choose their school at the admission time.
• Students applying for admission to the UST School of Business choose their Program during
the admission process.
Academic Year
• The Academic Year at UST includes two main semesters, a Fall Semester and a Spring Semester.
Some courses are also offered in the summer. However, taking courses during the Summer
Semester is optional for students.
• The regular semester length is 16-17 weeks including the final exam period.

• The Summer Semester is a compressed semester into 7-8 weeks including the final exams period.
• Start and end of semesters for each Academic Year are published in the UST Academic Calendar
which is posted on the ZC website.
Academic Load
• UST applies the Credit Hours system, in which a Credit Hour is a measuring unit for the student
workload.
• The weekly contact hours per Credit Hour depends on the class type as given in Table 2.
• The maximum approved course load in a regular semester (Fall/ Spring) is 18 Credit Hours.
• For the Summer Semester, students are allowed to register a maximum of 6 Credit Hours.
• No overload is allowed in the first semester the student is admitted to UST.
• Overload of up to 3 Credit Hours in a regular semester (Fall/ Spring) is allowed for students
with a CGPA of 3.0000 and above.
• Overload of up to 3 Credit Hours in a regular semester (Fall/ Spring) may be allowed for
students with a CGPA less than 3.0000 with approval from the Dean of Academic Affairs.
• A student enrolled for 12 Credit Hours or more is considered to be a full-time student.
• A student enrolled for less than 12 Credit Hours is considered a part time student and will not
be entitled to any UST enrollment certificate.

Academic Advisor
• Upon admission to UST, the Academic Advising Unit assigns to each student an Academic
Advisor from UST faculty members (Section 9).
• The initially assigned Academic Advisor counsels the student in academic matters until the
student declares his/her program.
• Upon program declaration, the student is assigned an Academic Advisor from the program
selected by the student.
• The role of the Academic Advisor includes the following:
– Ensuring that the student adheres to the calendar and undergraduate studies regulations.
– Helping the student in selecting courses.
– Providing guidance to the student in assembling the study plan.
• The Academic Advisor should maintain contact with the student through regular meetings
that are mutually agreed upon and should be accessible to the student to give advice and
constructive criticism.
Course Registration
8.8.1
Selecting Courses
• The student is required to carefully select courses from the university-scheduled course list for
the current semester.
• With the guidance of the Academic Advisor, the selection of courses should satisfy the University,
School, and Program requirements, noting that no course can be registered without passing
the prerequisite courses specified in the course catalog.

• Course Restriction:
A core curriculum course cannot be double counted for two curricula. However, a student may
use a maximum of only one core course to be double counted to fulfil the requirements of both
program core curriculum and minor curriculum.
8.8.2
Registering Courses
• Students must register for their courses during the registration period announced in the UST
Calendar for the Academic Year.
• Before registration, the student must clear any related holds (financial, advising, medical,
admission, etc.) to have access to online registration. A late fee will apply after the registration
deadline.
• UST reserves the right to require earlier completion of registration by pre-registration and
prepayment of tuition and other fees.
• The student must follow the instructions sent during the Advising/Registration period through
the university emails as “Announcements”.
• The student must make sure that he/she has avoided conflict between selected courses and
sections.
• The student must make sure to get the approval for alternative courses/sections in case a
section is closed, or no places are available.
8.8.3
Late Registration
• Students may not be permitted to register after the late registration period during the first
week of classes without written approval from each course instructor and the Program Director.
A fee will be charged for late registration after the first day of classes.
• New Students are allowed to pay late during the first week of the semester without a late
registration fee.
Requirements for Graduation
• Students enrolled in the School of Business (BUS) must satisfactorily complete a minimum of
130 Credit Hours.
• Out of the 130 Credit Hours, BUS students are required to successfully complete at least,
– 16 Credit Hours of University Requirements constituting a set of General Education courses
(Humanities, Social Sciences, etc.), which are designed to provide UST students with skills
and knowledge to develop an intellectual, well-rounded, and successful personal identity;
– 48 Credit Hours of School Requirements that provide students with skills and knowledge
essential to develop a successful BUS graduate; and
– 66 Credit Hours of Program Requirements.

at Zewail Cityبمدیـــــنة زویل
• As part of the above requirements, BUS students must undertake an independent capstone
project during the last semester of their program. The purpose of the project is to demonstrate
students’ abilities to practice in their chosen area of expertise and apply knowledge gained
from their academic and internship experiences. Topics are selected by groups of students
and approved by the program capstone project coordinator. Topics must be related to applied
problems and analysis should be based on an integrated approach.
• In addition to the above requirements, BUS students must successfully complete an internship
amounting to 6 weeks of full-time work or their equivalent of part time work. The students
should do their internship in the Summer semester of the Third year and must complete it
before graduation.
• To qualify for graduation, students must achieve an overall CGPA of at least 2.0000.
• Students must complete all requirements and graduate within a maximum of 8 years.
• Egyptian male students must complete the “Military Training Course” (مادة التربية العسكرية) as
per the Egyptian Law 46 for year 1973
8.10
Minimum Satisfactory Academic Performance
• The minimum academic requirement in any semester is getting a CGPA of 2.0000.
• A student who fails to meet this minimum requirement will be placed on probation. If the
student’s CGPA is below 2.0000 in two consecutive semesters, the student will be required to
withdraw from UST as explained in the next section.
8.11
Probation, Suspension, and Dismissal
8.11.1
Probation
• Students are given an academic warning and are placed on probation for failing to achieve a
CGPA of 2.0000.
• Students on a probation status are not permitted to register for more than 12 Credit Hours per
semester. They are also not allowed to participate in UST extracurricular activities.
• Students on probation have two consecutive warning semesters (WS1 and WS2) to improve
their CGPA to 2.0000 or above. In addition, students on probation must achieve a minimum
semester GPA of at least 2.0000 in the first warning semester (WS1).
• Failing to get out of probation leads to dismissal as illustrated in Figure 3.
8.11.2
Suspension
• Suspension is a disciplinary action that temporarily separates a student from the university due
to academic misconduct. It takes effect immediately upon the violation.
• During the suspension period, the student:
a. Cannot enroll in any courses
b. Cannot request or receive official university documents

c. Cannot take part in any university-related activities
• Suspension begins immediately and applies to all academic semesters, including Summer.
However, when counting the required suspension period (one semester or one year), only
main semesters (Fall and Spring) are considered. Summer does not count toward fulfilling the
suspension period.
8.11.3
Dismissal
• Academic dismissal terminates the student’s relationship with UST for unsatisfactory academic
performance. Students may not register in UST courses in this case and dismissal status is
recorded in their transcripts.
• The following categories of students are subject to dismissal for poor academic performance:
a. Students on probation who fail to achieve a minimum semester GPA of 2.0000 in the
courses taken during the first warning semester (WS1).
b. Students on probation who fail to achieve a minimum CGPA of 2.0000 in the two warning
semesters (including those who achieved a minimum semester GPA of 2.0000 in the first
probation semester WS1 but failed to achieve a minimum CGPA of 2.0000 at the end of
the second warning semester WS2).
c. Students whose study period expired and did not graduate within a maximum of 8 years.
8.12
Exclusion and Withdrawal
8.12.1
Exclusion from a Course
• The course instructor may give a written warning to any student who disregards or disrespects
the course, and with the approval of the Dean of Strategic Enrollment Management, the student
will be excluded from the course and will not be permitted to continue in the class.
• Exclusion from a course leaves the student with WF grade and with no right to file any petition
or fill out the “Course Survey” forms.
8.12.2
Withdrawing from Courses
• Students may Add/Drop/Withdraw from courses during the allowed periods announced in the
Academic Calendar. This can be done using the UST online portal.
• Students should note that withdrawing from courses may change their full-time/part-time status
(refer to Section 8.4 on Academic Load).
• If a student dropped a course before the Add/Drop deadline, the student will receive a full
refund of any paid tuition covering this course (except for any late registration fees), and the
dropped course will not show in the student’s transcript.
• After the Add/Drop deadline, students who want to withdraw from one or more courses are
required to submit a withdraw form through the online petitions portal.

of any paid tuition covering that course.
• No academic credit is given for courses with grades of W /WP/WF.
8.12.3
Withdrawal from UST and Gap Semesters
• Students who need a Gap Semester due to emergency circumstances should fill out a “Gap
Semester Form” as per the announced deadline in the Academic Calendar. Students must
obtain all approvals stated in the form.

• Students opting to withdraw from UST should first withdraw from any registered courses,
After that they should fill out a Withdrawal from UST form and to obtain all the required
approvals from the Program Director before submitting the form to the Office of Admission
and Registration.
• Students may apply for “Readmission” if they wish to return after one or more Gap Semesters.
• Readmission to UST is not granted automatically and will be subject to evaluation before
approval.
• Dropping all courses during the semester without applying for a Gap Semester (before the
deadline) is considered “Withdrawal” from UST and students are required to fill out a withdrawal
form and to obtain all the required approvals from the course instructors and the Program
Director before submitting the form to the Office of Admission and Registration.
8.13
Attendance Policy
• The purpose of the attendance policy is to ensure that the students benefit from the learning
opportunities offered through lectures and laboratories.
• Attendance in all academic activities is mandatory and it is the responsibility of the instructors
to track the students’ attendance.
• Students who are absent for more than 25% of the time in any course should not be permitted
to attend the final exam and should receive a WF grade, or could be allowed to attend the
final exam if he/she presents an approved justification for his/her absence, for example:
– In case of illness, a medical report should be submitted to the ZC clinic and approved by
their Senior Physician.
– A justified force majeure or emergency case.
• The UST administration may decide to offer a course or more using hybrid education methods
after receiving such a recommendation from the program that offers the course. In hybrid
education, the attendance can be 60 % on campus and 40 % online or with other percentages
based on the nature of the course. Percentages other than 60 %-40 % should be recommended
by the relevant program curriculum committee and approved by the UST Curriculum Committee
and by ZC Executive President.
8.14
Assessment Policy
Students’ academic performance in courses is evaluated throughout the semester. Course grades
are based on multiple assessment items that may include assignments, reports, projects, discussions,
quizzes, midterm exam(s), and a final exam. The final grade is posted on the student’s record.
8.14.1
Assessment Purpose
• Assessing students’ performance against course learning outcomes.
• Monitoring the effectiveness of the learning environment.
• Encouraging student learning

Assessment Weights Guidelines
• For each course, the instructor posts the final grades for students at the end of the semester.

8.14.3
Late Submission Policy
• The policy for late submission of coursework is left to the course instructor who may refuse to
accept late work, accept it with a deduction, or accept it without deduction based on a valid
excuse.
8.15
Exam Policy
8.15.1
Midterm Exams
• The midterm exams will be held during the first lecture of the scheduled exam week, unless
otherwise agreed with students. Any changes should be reported to the Program Director,
Faculty Administrative Assistants, and the Office of Admission and Registration.
• Midterm exams can be a case study, a written exam, or a project. The form of the exam should
be announced clearly to the students on the first day of classes.
• No makeup exams should be offered to students who missed the midterm exams with no
officially approved excuse.
8.15.2
Final Exam
• Final exams are given for classes during the Final Exams Period announced each semester by
the Office of Admission and Registration with the approval of the Dean of Strategic Enrollment
Management and the ZC Executive President.

• Program Directors are not authorized to change the scheduled date.
• Students must attend the Final Exam and achieve at least 35% of the final grade to pass the
course.
• Unapproved absence from the exam results in an “F” grade in the course.
• No makeup exams should be offered to students who missed the final exams with no officially
approved excuse. For officially approved excuses, student should apply for Incomplete grade.
8.15.3
Online Examination
• UST may decide to assess the students partially or completely online after receiving this
recommendation from the relevant program. The structure of the assessment is based on
the nature of the course. This should be recommended by the relevant program curriculum
committee and approved by UST curriculum committee and by the ZC Executive President.
• For online exam, a remote invigilation system should be applied (such as proctoring software
programs).
8.15.4
Exam Code of Conduct
• Examinees are expected to:
– Be available at the exam venue at least 15 minutes before the exam.
– Bring all the necessary exam materials; e.g., pens, pencils, calculators, etc.
– Turn off cell phones and put them in their bags with any other unauthorized materials at
the front or rear of the exam venue as instructed by the invigilator.
– Show their ZC ID when asked and to place it on their desks throughout the exam.
– Always comply with the instructions of the invigilator.
– Raise their hand if they have a question or need the supervisor’s help at any time during
the exam.
– Stop writing immediately when the invigilator announces the end of the exam.
– Continue sitting quietly until the invigilator gives them the permission to leave the exam
venue.
• Improper Examinee Conduct:
The Invigilator is authorized to dismiss any student from the exam for any of the following
reasons:
– Giving or receiving help during the exam.
– Attempting to take the exam for someone else.
– Disturbing other candidates during the exam.
– Communicating and discussing the exam content with other examinees during exam
sessions.

– Possessing or using photographic, recording, or transmission devices.
– Removing exam materials or notations of any kind from the exam venue.
– Refusal to comply with time allotments or exam administration procedure.
– Behaving in an unprofessional or discourteous manner when interacting with others.
– Threatening or verbally abusing invigilators responsible for curbing or reporting improper
conduct.
– Any other breach of conduct.

grades, and quality points.

8.17
Incomplete Work
• Undergraduate students who are unable to complete a course may submit a petition to postpone
the course final exam beyond the exam period, provided that these students achieved a minimum
of 60 % of the coursework score.
• Any student submitting an incomplete petition must include an approved justification for not
attending the final exam, for example:
– In case of illness, a medical report should be submitted to the ZC clinic and approved by
their Senior Physician.
– A justified force majeure or emergency case.
• Actions necessary for the removal of the “incomplete” are as follows:

– If a grade of I for “incomplete” is assigned, the students must make arrangements with
the instructor to take the makeup Final Exam within one month after the end of the
exams of the same incomplete semester.
– Failure to complete the course within the specified period causes the grade in that course
to be recorded as F signifying failure.
• A student can have a maximum of 5 “Incomplete” grades throughout their course of study.
• If students have one incomplete grade, their academic load limit will not be affected in the
following semester. If they have more than one incomplete grade, the Credit Hours of the
incomplete course will be included in their academic load for the following semester.
• Students who receive an incomplete grade(s) while on probation due to deficiency in their
overall grade point average will not be allowed to register the following semester. If, however,
they complete their incomplete work before the end of the late registration period, and are
academically eligible, they will be allowed to proceed with registration.
8.18
Repeating Courses
• Students are allowed to repeat failed courses and to repeat courses for grade improvement
subject to the restrictions indicated below.
• A “Course Repeat” form should be submitted to the Office of Admission and Registration for
review and approval. In case of failing to obtain a prior approval and/or repeating the course in
the form of an “added” course, a penalty will be applied, the course will be dropped, and the
tuition will not be refunded.
• All attempts for the course will be recorded in the student’s transcripts.
• The final grade to be used for GPA calculation is the latest grade.
• If the student withdrew from the repeated course, the prior grade will be used for GPA calculation.
A grade of W / WP / WF will appear in the student’s transcript for the withdrawn attempt.
8.18.1
Repeating a Course for Improvement
• Students are allowed to repeat courses for grade improvement subject to course availability and
resources considerations.
• During their study program, students may repeat a maximum of five different courses for grade
improvement.
• Students may repeat a course once for the purpose of improving their grades.
• Repeating a course for improvement is allowed only for courses in the same student level or
less by no more than two levels (i.e., a senior student cannot repeat a freshman course for
improvement).
8.18.2
Failed Course Repeat Policy
• Students are allowed to repeat failed courses for up to three times.
